proposals.title: Translating Addiction Neurocircuitry Biomarkers: A Functional MRI Study of Glutamatergic Engagement in Psychosis-Prone States
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research strongly implicates glutamatergic hypofunction, particularly at NMDA receptors, in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. However, in vivo biomarkers to quantify glutamatergic circuit engagement in patients are lacking. Concurrently, addiction neuroscience has robustly developed fMRI biomarkers (e.g., cue-reactivity, resting-state functional connectivity of corticostriatal circuits) to index drug-induced glutamatergic adaptations. This project aims to bridge these fields to create a novel translational tool for psychosis research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fMRI biomarkers of corticostriatal dysregulation, validated in cocaine addiction studies, will correlate with behavioral and symptomatic markers of psychosis-proneness in humans and will be modulated by pharmacological agents (e.g., rapastinel) known to rescue glutamatergic dysfunction in preclinical models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-validated cocaine cue-reactivity fMRI paradigm and a resting-state corticostriatal connectivity protocol for use in a human cohort stratified by psychosis-proneness (using the Schizotypal Personality Questionnaire). Concurrently, in a parallel preclinical arm, we will administer the same fMRI biomarker protocol to phencyclidine (PCP)-treated mice, a model of glutamatergic hypofunction, before and after treatment with the NMDA receptor glycine-site modulator rapastinel. Analyses will employ seed-based connectivity (e.g., ventral striatum, dorsal anterior cingulate cortex) and general linear modeling for task responses, using established pipelines (e.g., CONN toolbox, SPM12).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, translatable fMRI biomarker suite for assessing glutamatergic circuit integrity in psychosis, linking human symptomatology directly to a well-characterized neurobiological mechanism. It will also offer a direct method to evaluate the circuit-level effects of glutamatergic-property drugs, like rapastinel and NU-1223, bridging Dr. Meltzer's recent preclinical findings to human application.
proposals.novelty: While glutamatergic theories of psychosis are established, directly importing and validating fMRI biomarkers from addiction to quantify this dysfunction is a novel, mechanism-focused approach. The parallel human-preclinical design using identical imaging biomarkers is innovative for testing pharmacological rescue of circuit deficits.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Discovery of Novel Positive Allosteric Modulators of the NMDA Receptor for the Treatment of Cognitive and Negative Symptoms in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of atypical antipsychotics for positive symptoms, cognitive deficits and negative symptoms in schizophrenia remain largely untreated, representing a major unmet clinical need. Pre-2026 evidence strongly implicates hypofunction of the NMDA receptor in these symptoms, yet direct agonists are neurotoxic, and previous efforts to develop glycine-site modulators have shown limited clinical success. Fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD) offers a powerful, unbiased approach to identify novel chemical scaffolds that could modulate the NMDA receptor with greater specificity and therapeutic potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Fragment-based screening against the GluN1/GluN2B NMDA receptor will identify novel, chemically diverse small-molecule fragments that act as positive allosteric modulators (PAMs), which can be optimized into leads that rescue cognitive and negative symptom deficits in preclinical models of schizophrenia.
proposals.method: We will employ a biophysical fragment screening cascade against purified GluN1/GluN2B NMDA receptor protein. Primary screening will use surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to identify low molecular weight (<300 Da) fragments with confirmed binding. Hits will be validated and characterized using orthogonal assays, including fluorescence-based thermal shift assays and two-electrode voltage clamp electrophysiology in Xenopus oocytes to determine functional PAM activity. Promising fragment hits will be structurally elaborated using structure-guided design, informed by available NMDA receptor cryo-EM structures, and their efficacy will be tested in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, assessing novel object recognition and social interaction to model cognitive and negative symptoms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel chemical starting points for glutamatergic therapeutics targeting an intractable domain of schizophrenia pathology. It will provide proof-of-concept that FBDD can be successfully applied to complex ionotropic glutamate receptors, a target class where it has been underutilized. The resulting lead compounds will offer a new pharmacological strategy distinct from current D2/5-HT2A-centric or glycine-site approaches.
proposals.novelty: While FBDD is common in oncology and infectious disease, its application to ionotropic glutamate receptors for psychiatric drug discovery is novel. This approach moves beyond modifying known scaffolds (e.g., rapastinel analogs) to de novo discovery of novel chemotypes with potentially unique mechanisms and improved therapeutic profiles.

proposals.title: Functional Phenotyping of Glutamatergic Modulators in Patient-Derived Cortical Microcircuits: Linking In Vitro Network Dynamics to Treatment-Resistant Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of clozapine, a significant proportion of schizophrenia patients remain treatment-resistant, highlighting a critical need for novel therapeutics. Pre-2026 evidence strongly implicates glutamatergic dysfunction, particularly NMDAR hypofunction, in schizophrenia pathophysiology, but translating findings from rodent models to human patients remains a major bottleneck. Human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neuronal cultures offer a direct, patient-specific platform to study disease-relevant microcircuit abnormalities and drug responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cortical microcircuits derived from iPSCs of patients with treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS) will exhibit distinct, quantifiable deficits in network synchronization and bursting dynamics, and that these deficits will be selectively rescued by glutamatergic modulators (e.g., rapastinel-like agents) but not by typical antipsychotics.
proposals.method: We will generate cortical glutamatergic and GABAergic neurons from iPSCs of TRS patients (defined by prior clozapine use) and matched controls. Neurons will be co-cultured on multi-electrode arrays (MEAs) to form spontaneously active microcircuits. We will employ MEA recording to quantify network-level functional phenotypes, including mean firing rate, burst characteristics, and synchronization indices. These microcircuits will be pharmacologically challenged with a panel of compounds: the NMDAR positive allosteric modulator rapastinel, the atypical antipsychotic clozapine, and phencyclidine (PCP) to induce an NMDAR hypofunction state. Data analysis will use custom algorithms for burst detection and cross-correlation analysis of network synchrony.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, functional link between patient-specific cellular pathology and circuit-level dysfunction measurable in vitro. It will provide a quantitative platform for stratifying TRS based on neuronal network phenotypes and for screening glutamatergic compounds for personalized therapeutic potential. The findings will bridge the gap between rodent model pharmacology and human neurobiology, offering a novel biomarker strategy for treatment-resistant psychosis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Meltzer and others used rodent models (e.g., PCP-treated mice) to test glutamatergic drugs, this proposal directly tests these compounds on functional human neuronal networks derived from the actual patient population of greatest need—those with documented treatment resistance. The integration of patient-derived cells with high-content MEA phenotyping for drug screening in schizophrenia is a novel and clinically grounded approach.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Synergistic Efficacy of a Glycine Transporter-1 Inhibitor and a Metabotropic Glutamate Receptor 2/3 Negative Allosteric Modulator for Rescuing Cognitive and Social Deficits in a Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: The hypoglutamatergic hypothesis of schizophrenia, particularly NMDA receptor hypofunction, is a key pathophysiological model, but directly enhancing glutamate can precipitate excitotoxicity and psychosis. Current approaches, like GlyT1 inhibitors, aim to boost NMDA function indirectly, but their efficacy may be limited by compensatory feedback mechanisms and failure to address circuit-level dysregulation. A strategy that simultaneously enhances deficient signaling while preventing pathological excess could offer a superior therapeutic window.
proposals.hypothesis: Combining a GlyT1 inhibitor (to enhance NMDA receptor function via increased synaptic glycine) with a low-dose mGluR2/3 NAM (to selectively disinhibit presynaptic glutamate release at deficient synapses) will produce a synergistic rescue of cognitive and negative symptoms in the subchronic PCP mouse model without inducing behavioral or neurochemical markers of excitotoxicity, outperforming either monotherapy.
proposals.method: We will employ the established subchronic PCP (5 mg/kg/day for 7 days, 7-day washout) C57BL/6J mouse model. Mice will be treated with vehicle, the GlyT1 inhibitor bitopertin (3 mg/kg), the mGluR2/3 NAM RO-4491533 (1 mg/kg), or their combination. Behavioral testing will assess novel object recognition (memory), social interaction (negative symptom proxy), and locomotor activity. Ex vivo neurochemical analysis will measure glutamate and GABA levels in prefrontal cortex and hippocampal microdialysates. Immunohistochemistry for c-Fos will map neuronal activation, and Fluoro-Jade B staining will assess excitotoxic neuronal injury.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical preclinical proof-of-concept for a novel dual-mechanism pharmacological strategy targeting glutamatergic dysfunction in schizophrenia. It will delineate the behavioral and neurochemical interactions between GlyT1 inhibition and mGluR2/3 modulation, informing the development of next-generation treatments for cognitive and negative symptoms. The study will also establish safety parameters by directly testing for excitotoxicity, a major hurdle in glutamatergic drug development.
proposals.novelty: While GlyT1 inhibitors and mGluR2/3 NAMs have been studied separately, their combination is unexplored. The novelty lies in the hypothesis that a low-dose mGluR2/3 NAM can 'tune' glutamate release to synergize with GlyT1 inhibition, achieving enhanced efficacy without the toxicity associated with broad glutamate agonists or high-dose mGluR2/3 blockade.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Tolerability of Sub-441 mg Doses of Rapastinel for Acute Rescue of Cognitive and Psychotic Symptoms in a Phencyclidine Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Rapastinel (GLYX-13) has shown promise as a novel, rapid-acting antidepressant with NMDA receptor modulation, and recent pre-2026 work by Meltzer's group demonstrated its exceptionally prolonged rescue of memory deficits in a phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model of schizophrenia. However, the effective dose used in these studies (e.g., 10 mg/kg in mice, analogous to higher human doses) may not be optimal for minimizing potential side effects or for acute symptom rescue. Establishing a lower effective dose range is critical for translational development, improving tolerability, and understanding the dose-response relationship for glutamatergic modulators in psychosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that doses of rapastinel lower than the previously established effective dose (10 mg/kg, i.p.) will be sufficient to acutely rescue PCP-induced deficits in prepulse inhibition (PPI), novel object recognition (NOR), and social interaction, with a superior side-effect profile compared to the higher dose.
proposals.method: We will employ the established subchronic PCP (5 mg/kg, 14 days) mouse model. Following a washout period, mice will be acutely administered rapastinel at doses of 1, 3, and 10 mg/kg (i.p.), with vehicle and positive control (clozapine) groups. Behavioral testing for positive-like (PPI), negative-like (social interaction), and cognitive (NOR) symptoms will be conducted 24 and 48 hours post-injection. Data will be analyzed using mixed-design ANOVA with PCP treatment and rapastinel dose as between-subjects factors, followed by post-hoc Tukey tests. Side-effect profiling will include monitoring for locomotor hyperactivity and ataxia.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the minimal effective dose of rapastinel for acute symptom rescue in a validated schizophrenia model, providing crucial pre-clinical data for dose selection in future clinical trials. It will contribute to the field of glutamatergic therapeutics by clarifying the dose-response curve for rapid-acting NMDA receptor modulators in treating core schizophrenia symptom domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established the prolonged efficacy of a single high dose, this study systematically explores a lower, potentially more translatable and tolerable dose range for acute intervention, a strategy not yet reported for rapastinel in the context of psychotic symptoms.

proposals.title: Early Plasma Proteomic and Clinical Signatures Predict Day 8 Response to Aripiprazole Lauroxil in Schizophrenia: A Biomarker Discovery Study
proposals.motivation: Aripiprazole lauroxil is a long-acting injectable atypical antipsychotic crucial for managing schizophrenia, but clinical response is variable and often takes weeks to assess fully. Identifying early predictors of rapid response by day 8 would enable timely treatment adjustments, potentially improving outcomes and reducing the burden of prolonged psychosis. This aligns with the field's pre-2026 push for personalized medicine in psychiatry and builds on Dr. Meltzer's extensive work on treatment response and novel therapeutics in schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker signature, integrating baseline clinical/demographic variables with changes in specific plasma protein levels (e.g., inflammatory markers, neurotrophic factors) from baseline to day 3 post-injection, will significantly predict clinically meaningful improvement in Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS) total score by day 8 of aripiprazole lauroxil treatment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, open-label study in 80 patients with schizophrenia initiating aripiprazole lauroxil. Clinical assessments (PANSS, side effects) and blood draws will occur at baseline, day 3, and day 8. Plasma will be analyzed using a pre-2026, validated multiplex immunoassay panel targeting 40 proteins implicated in psychosis and antipsychotic response (e.g., BDNF, IL-6, CRP). We will employ machine learning (specifically, regularized regression like LASSO and Random Forest) to identify the most predictive features from the combined clinical and proteomic dataset for day 8 response (defined as ≥20% PANSS reduction). Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first preliminary biomarker profile for early response prediction to a specific long-acting injectable antipsychotic, aripiprazole lauroxil. It will advance the field toward actionable, early decision-support tools in clinical psychiatry. The findings may also shed light on the early biological mechanisms of partial D2 agonism, informing future drug development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has sought predictors for clozapine or generic treatment response, this proposal uniquely focuses on the critical early time window (day 8) for a specific long-acting injectable, leveraging Dr. Meltzer's expertise in translational models (e.g., PCP mice) to inform candidate protein selection in a clinical trial.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Emergence of Adverse Health Outcomes in First-Episode Psychosis: A Prospective, Multi-Domain Study to Disentangle Illness from Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: Patients with schizophrenia suffer from a significant burden of adverse health outcomes (AHOs), including metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, and premature mortality. By pre-2026, it is established that both the pathophysiology of psychotic illness and the long-term effects of antipsychotic medications contribute to this burden, but their relative contributions are confounded in chronic patient populations. Studying individuals at the first episode of psychosis (FEP) provides a critical window to observe the initial trajectory of AHOs before the effects of chronicity and prolonged polypharmacy obscure their origins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of AHOs, particularly metabolic and inflammatory dysregulation, are present at or shortly after the first episode of psychosis, preceding sustained exposure to antipsychotic treatment, and that their severity is associated with the acuity of positive and negative symptoms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective longitudinal study of 100 antipsychotic-naïve FEP patients and 50 matched healthy controls. At baseline, 6 months, and 12 months, we will comprehensively assess: 1) Core psychopathology (PANSS, SANS), 2) Metabolic parameters (fasting glucose, lipids, insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), waist circumference), 3) Systemic inflammation (high-sensitivity CRP, IL-6, TNF-α), and 4) Treatment details (chlorpromazine equivalents, specific agent(s)). Statistical analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to examine the temporal relationships between symptom domains, treatment initiation (focusing on atypical antipsychotics like clozapine, given Meltzer's expertise), and the emergence of AHOs, controlling for lifestyle factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, longitudinal data on the natural history of AHOs at illness onset, directly informing the debate on illness- versus medication-induced risk. It will identify specific, early biomarkers of AHO vulnerability, enabling targeted monitoring and preventive strategies. The findings will guide the development of early intervention protocols that aim to treat psychosis while mitigating long-term physical health risks from the outset.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Meltzer and others has extensively characterized AHOs in chronic or treatment-resistant schizophrenia, this proposal focuses exclusively on the antipsychotic-naïve, first-episode population to establish a causal timeline. It integrates detailed psychopathological assessment with multi-system physiological profiling from the point of diagnosis, a design not commonly employed in pre-2026 research on physical health in psychosis.

proposals.title: Early Prediction of Clozapine-Induced Agranulocytosis Using a Machine Learning Model Adapted from Oncology Pharmacovigilance
proposals.motivation: Clozapine remains the most effective antipsychotic for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, but its use is severely limited by the risk of agranulocytosis, a potentially fatal drop in neutrophil count. Current monitoring mandates weekly blood draws for six months, a burdensome process that delays effective treatment and reduces adherence. An accurate predictive model could enable risk-stratified monitoring, improving safety and access to this critical therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models, specifically those developed to predict hematological adverse events from oncology drugs, can be adapted using pre-treatment and early-treatment clinical data to identify patients at high risk for clozapine-induced agranulocytosis (CIA) earlier than standard monitoring.
proposals.method: We will adapt a gradient boosting machine (GBM) architecture, proven in pre-2026 oncology pharmacovigilance for predicting chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. Using de-identified data from the Clozapine Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) program and linked electronic health records, we will engineer features including baseline complete blood count (CBC) trends, demographics, concomitant medications, and early (first 4-week) CBC trajectories. The model will be trained to predict agranulocytosis occurring within the first 6 months of treatment, with performance validated via temporal cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically actionable risk prediction tool for CIA, potentially transforming monitoring protocols from a universal, fixed schedule to a personalized, risk-based approach. It would directly address a major barrier to clozapine use, aligning with Dr. Meltzer's long-standing research on optimizing outcomes in treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacovigilance AI is established in oncology, its application to predict a specific, rare antipsychotic side effect by translating algorithmic frameworks from a different drug class is novel. This approach moves beyond post-hoc signal detection to proactive, individual risk prediction for a uniquely challenging adverse event.

proposals.title: Targeting NMDAR-Dysfunction-Induced Predictive Coding Errors with Adaptive Cognitive Remediation: A Translational Study in a Phencyclidine Mouse Model and Patients with Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: The N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) hypofunction model is a leading pathophysiological theory of schizophrenia, strongly supported by phencyclidine (PCP) models, but its translation into effective, mechanism-informed cognitive treatments remains a critical unmet need. Current cognitive remediation strategies are broad and not explicitly linked to the specific computational failures—such as aberrant predictive coding—that are theorized to arise from NMDAR dysfunction in fronto-temporal-thalamic circuits. Bridging this gap is essential for developing targeted, biologically grounded interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subchronic PCP-induced NMDAR hypofunction in mice and cognitive deficits in schizophrenia patients manifest as quantifiable, circuit-specific distortions in predictive coding algorithms, and that these distortions can be selectively recalibrated using adaptive cognitive tasks designed to engage and normalize these specific computational processes.
proposals.method: We will first characterize predictive coding signatures using EEG (mismatch negativity, P300) and magnetic resonance spectroscopy (glutamate/GABA levels) in a subchronic PCP mouse model and in patients with schizophrenia. We will then develop and validate adaptive cognitive tasks (e.g., probabilistic sequence learning, oddball paradigms with varying surprise) that manipulate predictive uncertainty. In mice, tasks will be implemented in touchscreen operant chambers; in patients, via computerized platforms. Real-time EEG neurofeedback will be integrated to create a closed-loop system where task difficulty adapts based on neural signatures of prediction error, ensuring target engagement of the hypothesized dysfunctional circuits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct translational framework linking the NMDAR hypofunction model to a novel, circuit-specific cognitive remediation strategy. It will yield a set of validated neural biomarkers (EEG/MRS) for tracking target engagement and a prototype for closed-loop, adaptive cognitive training that can be tested in future clinical trials for cognitive impairment in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond broad pharmacological analogy or generic cognitive training by using a computational psychiatry framework to design interventions that directly target the algorithmic distortions predicted by the NMDAR hypofunction theory, creating a precise, biomarker-driven remediation paradigm.

proposals.title: A Host-Directed Cognitive Therapy for Schizophrenia: Targeting Stable Neural Algorithms to Circumvent NMDAR Hypofunction
proposals.motivation: Cognitive impairment in schizophrenia, linked to N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) hypofunction, remains a major unmet need. Direct pharmacological correction of NMDAR function has proven difficult, mirroring challenges in virology where targeting a mutable pathogen often fails. This proposal adapts the successful virology strategy of host-directed therapy, shifting focus from the dysfunctional 'pathogen' (NMDAR) to strengthening the stable 'host' (alternative cognitive systems).
proposals.hypothesis: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia can be more robustly improved by identifying and therapeutically strengthening specific, stable cognitive algorithms and their neural substrates that are less dependent on optimal NMDAR function, rather than by directly targeting the receptor.
proposals.method: We will first use a computational phenotyping approach, applying drift-diffusion modeling and reinforcement learning algorithms to behavioral data from patients with schizophrenia and subchronic phencyclidine (PCP)-treated mice to identify specific decision-making or learning parameters preserved despite NMDAR antagonism. Based on these identified 'stable host algorithms,' we will then develop a targeted cognitive training protocol in mice, using touchscreen-based assays (e.g., pairwise visual discrimination/reversal) designed to explicitly engage these circuits. Concurrently, we will apply repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) protocols, guided by pre-2026 connectivity targets like the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex-hippocampal network, to potentiate these alternative pathways and assess synergy with training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistically grounded framework for cognitive remediation by defining compensatory neural algorithms. It will yield a preclinical proof-of-concept for a combined cognitive training and neuromodulation strategy that is robust to NMDAR dysfunction, directly informing future clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual inversion, adapting a host-directed therapy framework from virology to neuropsychiatry, and in its method of using computational modeling to identify specific, stable cognitive targets for intervention rather than broadly training impaired domains.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Bridging of Ketamine-Induced Cortical Disinhibition to Hippocampal Pattern Separation Deficits: A Multi-Scale Computational and Behavioral Study in a Rodent Model of Psychosis
proposals.motivation: Ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, induces psychosis-like states and episodic memory impairments relevant to schizophrenia, but the causal chain from its molecular action to specific cognitive computations remains opaque. Current research, including Dr. Meltzer's work with phencyclidine (PCP) models and novel therapeutics like NU-1223, often correlates drug effects with behavioral readouts without a formal, testable model of the intervening neural network dynamics. A framework that quantitatively links pharmacology to circuit-level computation is critically needed to advance mechanistic psychopharmacology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ketamine's primary action of NMDA-R blockade on cortical GABAergic interneurons leads to cortical disinhibition, which disrupts the dynamic coordination of hippocampal-cortical networks, specifically degrading the pattern separation computation essential for episodic memory fidelity, and that this disruption can be captured and predicted by a biologically-constrained multi-scale computational model.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale model integrating: 1) a pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) module simulating ketamine's NMDA-R blockade and resultant cortical disinhibition; 2) a spiking neural network model of the hippocampal dentate gyrus/CA3 circuit performing pattern separation/completion, whose excitatory/inhibitory balance is modulated by the PK/PD output; and 3) a behavioral readout module translating network activity into quantitative predictions for the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) task. Model parameters will be constrained by in vivo electrophysiology (local field potentials in mPFC and hippocampus) and NOR data from C57BL/6J mice treated with subanesthetic ketamine (5-10 mg/kg) versus saline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for tracing a drug's effect from molecular target to cognitive symptom, moving beyond descriptive correlation. It will generate specific, testable predictions about network states underlying memory deficits, offering a new tool for evaluating and refining the mechanisms of novel antipsychotic candidates, such as 5-HT2cR agonists, that may correct these dysregulated dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, causal parameterization of a computational memory model by the downstream synaptic consequences of a specific molecular pharmacology (ketamine's NMDA-R blockade), rather than using models as purely descriptive correlates or abstract cognitive constructs.

proposals.title: Causal Network Mapping of Ketamine's Rapid Antidepressant Action via Closed-Loop Perturbation and Real-Time Circuit Analysis in Rodents
proposals.motivation: Despite ketamine's proven rapid antidepressant efficacy, its mechanism remains incompletely understood, hindering the development of safer, more reliable treatments. Pre-2026 research has identified multiple candidate pathways (e.g., NMDA receptor blockade, AMPA receptor throughput, mTOR activation, BDNF release) but treats them largely in isolation, failing to define their necessary and sufficient interactions within intact neural circuits. A systems-level causal model is urgently needed to move beyond correlative observations to a predictive understanding of the therapeutic action.
proposals.hypothesis: Ketamine's rapid antidepressant effect is not caused by a single linear pathway but emerges from a specific, minimal causal network of interacting molecular events (e.g., disinhibition via GABAergic interneurons, glutamate burst, synaptic protein synthesis) that converge to produce sustained plasticity in defined mood-relevant circuits (e.g., medial prefrontal cortex to hippocampus or amygdala).
proposals.method: We will employ a closed-loop experimental system in mice subjected to chronic stress, combining simultaneous optogenetic/chemogenetic perturbations of candidate nodes (e.g., PV+ interneurons, VTA dopamine neurons) with real-time monitoring via in vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP) in mood circuits and local field potential recordings. Following sub-anesthetic ketamine administration, high-dimensional time-series data will be analyzed using causal inference algorithms (e.g., PCMCI for time-lagged discovery) and machine learning (e.g., graph neural networks) to infer the necessary causal structure and interactions leading to behavioral rescue in forced swim and sucrose preference tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically-derived, predictive model of the minimal causal network required for ketamine's action, identifying robust control points for intervention. It will shift the field from a focus on single triggers to a systems pharmacology perspective, directly informing the development of targeted, circuit-based therapies for treatment-resistant depression and potentially for cognitive deficits in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: The integration of multi-node targeted perturbations with real-time circuit monitoring and causal ML analysis in a closed-loop design moves beyond static, single-pathway studies. This approach is novel in its direct aim to disentangle necessity and sufficiency within the dynamic, interacting system, building upon but fundamentally extending pre-2026 pharmacological and imaging work.

proposals.title: Selective Antagonism of GluN2C-Containing NMDA Receptors Reverses Cognitive and Negative Symptom Deficits in a Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model Without Inducing Psychotomimetic Effects
proposals.motivation: NMDA receptor (NMDAR) hypofunction is a core pathophysiological mechanism in schizophrenia, but broad-spectrum NMDAR antagonists like ketamine induce psychosis. Pre-2026 evidence suggests distinct NMDAR subunit compositions (e.g., GluN2A, GluN2B, GluN2C, GluN2D) mediate different neural functions. Non-selective blockade causes a mix of therapeutic and adverse effects, limiting clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selective pharmacological antagonism of GluN2C-containing NMDARs will rescue cognitive deficits and negative symptoms in a validated mouse model of schizophrenia, while avoiding the psychotomimetic and motor side effects associated with broader NMDAR blockade.
proposals.method: We will employ a medicinal chemistry approach to develop novel, high-affinity negative allosteric modulators (NAMs) selective for GluN2C over other GluN2 subunits, guided by pre-2026 crystal structure data and computational docking (using Schrödinger Suite). Candidate compounds will be tested in vitro for subunit selectivity using Xenopus oocyte electrophysiology. The lead compound will be evaluated in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, assessing novel object recognition (memory), social interaction (negative symptoms), and locomotor activity. Its effects will be compared to the non-selective antagonist MK-801 and the pro-cognitive agent rapastinel.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept that subunit-specific NMDAR antagonism is a viable strategy for schizophrenia treatment. It will identify a potential novel pharmacological target (GluN2C) for cognitive and negative symptoms and generate a selective tool compound for future neurobiological studies of NMDAR function.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Meltzer's group and others has focused on NMDAR *potentiation* (e.g., rapastinel) or broad 5-HT2cR modulation, this proposal innovatively targets *selective inhibition* of a specific NMDAR subtype to dissect therapeutic from adverse effect pathways, a strategy not yet clinically explored for schizophrenia.

proposals.title: Inverse Pharmacological Inference of NMDAR Signaling States from Effective Cognitive Enhancers in a Phencyclidine Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia remain a major unmet therapeutic need. While NMDAR hypofunction is a central pathophysiological model, directly targeting the receptor has proven difficult. An inverse approach, starting from compounds with proven pro-cognitive efficacy in validated models, could reveal the specific downstream NMDAR signaling perturbations that must be corrected, offering new, more tractable targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Effective cognitive enhancers with diverse primary molecular targets (e.g., rapastinel, NU-1223) converge on a common set of restored NMDAR-mediated synaptic signaling pathways and plasticity metrics in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of phencyclidine-treated mice.
proposals.method: We will treat subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mice with rapastinel (a GlyT1 inhibitor/modulator) and NU-1223 (a 5-HT2cR agonist-like compound), both with established long-term cognitive rescue in this lab's model. Using ex vivo brain slices from treated mice, we will employ a multi-level electrophysiological and biochemical assay panel: 1) whole-cell patch-clamp to measure NMDAR current kinetics and Mg2+ sensitivity, 2) field potential recordings for LTP/LTD induction thresholds, and 3) quantitative Western blotting of key NMDAR signaling nodes (e.g., phosphorylation states of GluN2B, ERK, CREB). A computational Bayesian network analysis will then infer the minimal set of signaling perturbations most predictive of cognitive rescue across both treatments.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the generic 'NMDAR hypofunction' concept by defining a specific, quantifiable signature of 'correctable' NMDAR dysfunction. It will identify which downstream signaling elements are critical, common targets for cognitive enhancement, prioritizing them for future drug development in schizophrenia and related disorders.
proposals.novelty: Instead of testing NMDAR hypotheses directly, we use effective treatments as probes to reverse-engineer the requisite functional state of the NMDAR system, a novel inference strategy in psychiatric neuroscience. The integration of two mechanistically distinct cognitive enhancers strengthens the inference of convergent, essential pathways.

proposals.title: Efficacy of a Neutral 5-HT2A Antagonist Combined with 5-HT2C Agonism and AMPA Potentiation in a Preclinical Model of Schizophrenia: Challenging the Inverse Agonism Paradigm
proposals.motivation: Current atypical antipsychotics like clozapine and NU-1223 are thought to require 5-HT2A receptor inverse agonism for efficacy, particularly against positive symptoms. This assumption limits drug design and may overlook potentially effective compounds with cleaner receptor profiles and fewer side effects. Testing whether neutral antagonism at 5-HT2A is sufficient when combined with other pro-cognitive mechanisms addresses a core, unresolved question in antipsychotic pharmacology.
proposals.hypothesis: A neutral 5-HT2A receptor antagonist, when co-administered with a 5-HT2C receptor agonist (e.g., NU-1223 analog) and an AMPA potentiator (e.g., rapastinel analog), will effectively rescue phencyclidine-induced deficits in prepulse inhibition (positive symptoms) and novel object recognition (cognitive/negative symptoms) in mice, without requiring intrinsic inverse agonist activity at the 5-HT2A receptor.
proposals.method: We will employ the established subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model. Mice will be treated with a selective neutral 5-HT2A antagonist (e.g., volinanserin/MDL-100,907), alone and in combination with a simplified alstonine analog (5-HT2C agonist) and a rapastinel analog. Primary outcomes will be prepulse inhibition (PPI) of the startle response and novel object recognition (NOR) memory. Receptor occupancy and functional profiles (inverse vs. neutral antagonism) of the test compounds will be confirmed using in vitro cell-based assays measuring phospholipase C and arrestin recruitment. Data will be analyzed using ANOVA with post-hoc tests comparing combination therapy to monotherapies and vehicle.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a foundational assumption in antipsychotic drug development. It could validate a new strategy for designing multi-target antipsychotics with potentially improved tolerability by avoiding constitutive silencing of 5-HT2A signaling. A positive result would significantly expand the chemical space for future antipsychotic candidates.
proposals.novelty: While combination therapies are studied, explicitly decoupling 5-HT2A neutral antagonism from inverse agonism in a multi-mechanism framework is novel. It challenges the direct extrapolation of clozapine's mechanism to all effective agents and leverages Meltzer's recent work on NU-1223 and rapastinel to create a targeted polypharmacy test.

proposals.title: Dissociable Serotonergic Mechanisms Underlying Phencyclidine-Induced Hyperlocomotion and Novel Object Recognition Deficits in Mice: A Pharmacological and Receptor-Specific Investigation
proposals.motivation: The subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model is widely used to study both positive-like (hyperlocomotion) and cognitive (e.g., NOR deficit) symptoms of schizophrenia. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab with NU-1223, shows that 5-HT2C receptor agonism can rescue both symptom domains. However, it remains unclear if these therapeutic effects stem from a unified serotonergic mechanism correcting a core PCP-induced dysfunction, or from distinct, parallel pathways. Clarifying this is critical for developing more precise, symptom-specific pharmacotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the serotonergic mechanisms rescuing PCP-induced hyperlocomotion and NOR deficits are pharmacologically dissociable, involving distinct receptor subtypes (e.g., 5-HT2A vs. 5-HT2C) and/or brain circuits, rather than a single unified pathway.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subjects, longitudinal design in C57BL/6J mice subjected to subchronic PCP. Following baseline behavioral characterization, mice will receive acute challenges with selective pharmacological agents: a 5-HT2C agonist (e.g., WAY-163909), a 5-HT2A antagonist (e.g., M100907), and NU-1223. Locomotor activity will be quantified in automated open fields, and cognitive function assessed using the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) task with a 24-hour delay. Data will be analyzed using repeated-measures ANOVA to compare drug effects across behavioral domains, followed by post-hoc tests to identify dissociations. Receptor specificity will be confirmed using selective antagonists prior to agonist administration.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the unity of serotonergic rescue in a key preclinical model, providing a clearer neuropharmacological map for treating distinct symptom clusters. It will inform the targeted development of next-generation antipsychotics, such as refined analogs of alstonine or rapastinel-like agents, by identifying whether a single compound can optimally address both symptom domains or if combination therapies are warranted.
proposals.novelty: While prior work demonstrates efficacy of serotonergic drugs in the PCP model, no study has systematically compared the receptor pharmacology of rescue across locomotor and cognitive domains within the same animal cohort, which is essential for determining mechanistic unity or dissociation.

proposals.title: Quantitative Contribution of 5-HT1A Receptor Agonism to the Pro-Cognitive Effects of Atypical Antipsychotics in the Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Atypical antipsychotics (APDs) like clozapine and aripiprazole possess 5-HT1A receptor agonist properties, which are hypothesized to contribute to their efficacy against cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, a core symptom domain poorly addressed by typical APDs. However, the precise quantitative contribution of 5-HT1A activation to cognitive recovery, independent of other receptor actions (e.g., D2 antagonism, 5-HT2A antagonism), remains undefined, limiting the rational design of more targeted cognitive therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant and quantifiable portion of the efficacy of atypical APDs in rescuing novel object recognition (NOR) deficits in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model is mediated specifically through 5-HT1A receptor agonism.
proposals.method: We will use the established subchronic PCP mouse model to induce robust NOR deficits. Atypical APDs with known 5-HT1A activity (e.g., aripiprazole, clozapine) will be administered alone and in combination with the selective 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635. Using a full factorial design, we will quantify the reversal of NOR deficits. The specific contribution of 5-HT1A agonism will be calculated as the difference in efficacy (d prime or discrimination index) between the APD-alone condition and the APD + WAY-100635 condition. Parallel experiments will assess the effects of a selective 5-HT1A agonist (e.g., flesinoxan) as a positive control.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of the 5-HT1A receptor's contribution to the pro-cognitive action of specific atypical APDs in a validated translational model. It will directly inform the development of novel pharmacotherapies by clarifying whether a 'pure' 5-HT1A strategy is sufficient or if polypharmacology is necessary for optimal cognitive benefit in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While 5-HT1A agonism is recognized as a component of atypical APD action, this proposal moves beyond correlation to causally and quantitatively dissect its specific contribution to cognitive recovery using receptor-selective pharmacological blockade within a well-characterized preclinical model of schizophrenia-related cognitive dysfunction.

proposals.title: In Vivo Microdialysis Assessment of Serotonin and Glutamate Dynamics in the Medial Prefrontal Cortex During NMDA Antagonist Challenge and Rescue by a 5-HT2cR Agonist-Like Compound in Mice
proposals.motivation: The NMDA receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia is central to understanding positive, negative, and cognitive symptoms. Preclinical models using NMDA antagonists like phencyclidine (PCP) are standard, but the real-time neurochemical interactions, particularly between serotonin (5-HT) and glutamate systems, during the induction and rescue of psychotic-like states remain poorly characterized. Clarifying this interaction is critical for developing novel treatments, especially given Dr. Meltzer's recent work on compounds like NU-1223 that target 5-HT2c receptors to rescue PCP-induced deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subchronic PCP administration will induce a dysregulated, dissociated state of prefrontal cortical serotonin and glutamate release, and that subsequent treatment with the 5-HT2cR agonist-like compound NU-1223 will restore a coordinated, physiological pattern of neurotransmission, correlating with behavioral rescue.
proposals.method: We will employ in vivo microdialysis in the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) of freely moving C57BL/6 mice. Following subchronic PCP (or saline) regimen, probes will be implanted and baseline extracellular levels of 5-HT and glutamate will be measured via HPLC with electrochemical and fluorometric detection. Mice will then receive an acute PCP challenge, followed by systemic administration of NU-1223 or vehicle, with continuous dialysate sampling. A separate cohort will receive rapastinel as a positive control. Data will be analyzed using two-way repeated measures ANOVA to compare neurotransmitter dynamics across treatment groups and time points.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, real-time neurochemical profile of the 5-HT-glutamate interaction during the PCP model's psychotic-like state and its pharmacological rescue. It will mechanistically validate the action of 5-HT2cR-targeting compounds like NU-1223 by linking behavioral efficacy to specific neurotransmitter dynamics. The findings will strengthen the rationale for modulating serotonin to correct glutamatergic dysfunction in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While microdialysis has been used to study glutamate or serotonin separately in schizophrenia models, simultaneously monitoring both neurotransmitters in real-time during both the induction (by PCP) and rescue (by a novel 5-HT2cR agent) of the phenotype is novel. This directly tests the interaction implied by Dr. Meltzer's prior behavioral pharmacology work.

proposals.title: Computational Modeling of Optimal Atypical Antipsychotic Receptor Occupancy Profiles for Reversal of NMDA Receptor Hypofunction in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: The NMDA receptor hypofunction model, validated by phencyclidine (PCP) administration in mice, is a critical preclinical model for schizophrenia, capturing cognitive deficits and positive/negative symptoms. Current atypical antipsychotics show variable efficacy, likely due to their complex polypharmacology at serotonin (5-HT) and dopamine (D2) receptors. A rational, quantitative framework to define optimal multi-receptor engagement for reversing NMDA antagonist effects is lacking, hindering the targeted development of novel therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, quantifiable ratio of occupancy at 5-HT2A, D2, and 5-HT1A receptors, derived from computational modeling of preclinical efficacy data, will define an optimal profile for rescuing behavioral deficits induced by NMDA receptor antagonism.
proposals.method: We will employ a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) modeling approach. Behavioral data (e.g., novel object recognition, prepulse inhibition) from published and in-house studies (e.g., using PCP-treated mice and compounds like NU-1223, clozapine, rapastinel) will be integrated with known in vitro receptor binding affinities (Ki). Using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM), we will fit a multi-receptor occupancy model to the efficacy data to estimate the relative weights and optimal occupancy ratios for 5-HT2A, D2, and 5-HT1A receptors required for symptom rescue.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive, quantitative model linking multi-receptor occupancy to in vivo efficacy in the NMDA hypofunction model. It will offer a principled method for ranking existing atypical antipsychotics and for guiding the design of new molecules with optimized polypharmacology for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, directly informing drug discovery pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While receptor occupancy is commonly measured, modeling an *optimal ratio* of occupancies across multiple receptors specifically for reversing NMDA antagonist effects is a novel, systems-level approach that moves beyond single-target thinking and empirical compound screening.

proposals.title: Rescue of Cognitive and Behavioral Deficits in a Phencyclidine Mouse Model via Targeted Environmental Enrichment and Cognitive Remediation, Independent of Direct Neurotransmitter System Engagement
proposals.motivation: Current pharmacotherapies for schizophrenia, including atypical antipsychotics and novel agents like NU-1223 and rapastinel, primarily target neurotransmitter systems (e.g., 5-HT2c, NMDA). While effective, this reinforces a neurochemical-centric model of treatment. It remains unknown whether the core cognitive and symptomatic deficits can be durably rescued through purely non-pharmacological, experience-dependent neural plasticity, challenging the necessity of direct receptor engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, combined intervention of environmental enrichment (EE) and computerized cognitive remediation (CCR) will induce sufficient neural compensation and behavioral sensitization to rescue PCP-induced deficits in memory and schizophrenia-relevant behaviors in mice, without any concomitant pharmacological manipulation.
proposals.method: We will use the established subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model. Following PCP regimen, mice will be randomly assigned to: 1) Standard housing (SH), 2) SH + CCR (touchscreen-based tasks for attention/working memory), 3) Complex EE (social, physical, sensory stimuli), and 4) EE + CCR. CCR will utilize the Bussey-Saksida touchscreen system with pairwise visual discrimination and reversal learning tasks. Primary outcomes will be novel object recognition (NOR) and social interaction tests, assessed longitudinally over 8 weeks. Brain tissue will be analyzed for synaptic markers (PSD-95, synaptophysin) and BDNF levels post-mortem.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct preclinical evidence that experience-dependent plasticity alone can reverse key deficits in a pharmacological model of schizophrenia. It will strengthen the theoretical basis for non-pharmacological interventions by demonstrating their mechanistic sufficiency at a neural systems level. The findings could inform the development of more potent behavioral therapies for cognitive negative symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While environmental enrichment has been studied broadly, its targeted combination with touchscreen cognitive remediation to specifically 'rescue' deficits in a standard pharmacological model, explicitly framed as an alternative to pharmacotherapy, is novel. This directly tests the primacy of neurotransmitter-targeting approaches by attempting to replicate the functional outcomes of drugs like NU-1223 and rapastinel through purely psychological/behavioral pathways.

proposals.title: Divergent Neural Adaptations in the Prefrontal Cortex: Chronic Social Defeat Stress vs. Repeated Phencyclidine Exposure in a Mouse Model of Schizophrenia-Relevant Pathology
proposals.motivation: Preclinical models of schizophrenia often use repeated exposure to NMDA receptor antagonists like phencyclidine (PCP) or chronic stress paradigms, both of which can induce cognitive deficits and social withdrawal. These are frequently treated as interchangeable proxies for environmental and pharmacological induction of psychosis-relevant states. However, it is unknown whether the underlying neural circuit and molecular adaptations are fundamentally equivalent, which has critical implications for translating preclinical findings to targeted treatments for schizophrenia spectrum disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic social defeat stress and repeated subchronic PCP administration, while producing similar behavioral phenotypes (e.g., cognitive deficits, negative symptoms), will induce distinct and non-overlapping patterns of synaptic protein expression, dendritic spine morphology, and functional connectivity within the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) and its projections to the hippocampus.
proposals.method: C57BL/6J mice will undergo either 10 days of chronic social defeat stress or a subchronic PCP regimen (5 mg/kg/day for 7 days). Behavioral profiling will assess working memory (Y-maze), social interaction, and prepulse inhibition. Brains will be processed for: 1) Quantitative mass spectrometry-based proteomics of mPFC synaptosomes to compare synaptic adaptation signatures; 2) High-resolution confocal imaging of Golgi-stained mPFC pyramidal neurons for spine density and morphology analysis; 3) Ex vivo electrophysiology to measure mPFC-to-ventral hippocampus pathway plasticity. Data integration will use multivariate analysis (e.g., PCA, PLS-DA) to identify discriminant patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a foundational assumption in psychiatric neuroscience, clarifying whether stress- and drug-induced models converge on a common pathological endpoint. It will provide a detailed map of divergent molecular and cellular targets, informing more precise model selection for studying specific symptom domains (e.g., cognitive vs. negative symptoms) and for screening novel compounds like NU-1223 or rapastinel.
proposals.novelty: While both models are widely used, a direct, multi-level comparison of their neural substrates has not been systematically conducted. The novelty lies in moving beyond behavioral equivalence to rigorously test for mechanistic divergence at the synaptic and circuit levels, guided by Dr. Meltzer's expertise in novel therapeutics and preclinical models.

proposals.title: Distinct Neuroadaptations in Monoamine and Glutamate Systems Underlie Compensatory versus Sensitizing Responses to Chronic Atypical Antipsychotic Exposure in a Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Atypical antipsychotics like clozapine produce variable long-term outcomes, with some patients showing sustained improvement (compensation) and others developing treatment resistance or supersensitivity (sensitization). The distinct neural adaptations underlying these divergent trajectories remain poorly defined, hindering the development of personalized, more effective treatments for schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic administration of an atypical antipsychotic (e.g., clozapine) in a phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model will induce two distinct neurobiological profiles: a 'compensation' profile characterized by restored glutamatergic (e.g., mGluR2/3, NMDA receptor function) and serotonergic (5-HT1A/2C) homeostasis, and a 'sensitization' profile marked by persistent dopaminergic hyperactivity and dysregulated endocannabinoid (eCB)-mediated synaptic plasticity.
proposals.method: We will use the subchronic PCP mouse model, followed by chronic clozapine treatment. Mice will be stratified into 'compensator' and 'sensitizer' phenotypes based on longitudinal assessments of cognitive deficits (novel object recognition) and psychotic-like symptoms (prepulse inhibition, locomotor sensitivity to acute MK-801 challenge). Ex vivo molecular and electrophysiological analyses will then be performed on prefrontal cortex and striatal tissue from each phenotype. We will quantify receptor and signaling protein expression (Western blot, radioligand binding), measure eCB (anandamide, 2-AG) levels (LC-MS), and assess synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD) in corticostriatal circuits, with a focus on cannabinoid CB1R and 5-HT2CR modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will define specific, measurable neurobiological signatures that predict long-term treatment outcome, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' model of antipsychotic action. It will identify novel targets (e.g., specific eCB or 5-HT2CR mechanisms) for adjunctive therapies to promote compensatory pathways and prevent sensitization, directly informing strategies for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines acute drug effects, this proposal uniquely links longitudinal behavioral phenotyping to multi-system neurochemical profiling to dissect divergent adaptation *trajectories*, integrating the investigator's expertise in PCP models, 5-HT2CR agonists, and clozapine's long-term outcomes.

proposals.title: Predicting Optimal Lurasidone Dose from Early Symptom Trajectory: A Machine Learning Approach to Personalized Treatment in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Dosing of atypical antipsychotics like lurasidone remains a trial-and-error process, often leading to prolonged periods of suboptimal treatment and increased risk of side effects. Pre-2026 research, including Meltzer's work with novel compounds in mouse models, highlights the importance of early treatment response as a predictor of long-term outcome. A method to predict the required dose from early response markers would directly address the clinical challenge of personalizing antipsychotic therapy, potentially improving outcomes and reducing the burden of treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: Early changes in positive and negative symptom scores, combined with baseline clinical and demographic variables, contain sufficient information to accurately predict the stable, effective dose of lurasidone for an individual patient.
proposals.method: We will analyze a longitudinal dataset of schizophrenia patients treated with lurasidone, capturing PANSS scores at baseline and weeks 1, 2, and 4. The target variable is the stabilized dose at week 12. We will employ a gradient boosting regression model (XGBoost) to predict dose, using features derived from early symptom trajectories (e.g., rate of change, area under the curve), baseline PANSS subscores, age, weight, and prior treatment history. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validated mean absolute error (MAE) in dose prediction and compared against a clinical heuristic baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept, data-driven tool to assist clinicians in dose titration, moving beyond fixed-dose or weight-based protocols. It will establish which early response markers are most predictive of final dose, informing future clinical trial designs for personalized medicine in psychosis. The model framework could be adapted for other atypical antipsychotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Meltzer and others has modeled response *to* a dose in animal and human studies, this proposal inverts the problem to predict dose *from* response, a clinically critical but understudied inverse problem in psychopharmacology. It applies contemporary machine learning methods to a rich, longitudinal clinical dataset specifically for lurasidone dosing.

proposals.title: Dissociating Primary Mechanisms of Atypicality: A Preclinical Investigation of Strong D2 Antagonism Combined with 5-HT1A Agonism as a Sufficient Mechanism for Antipsychotic Efficacy with Reduced Extrapyramidal Symptoms
proposals.motivation: The dominant pre-2026 model for atypical antipsychotic drug (APD) action posits that 5-HT2A receptor antagonism, which mitigates D2 blockade-induced extrapyramidal side effects (EPS), is a primary mechanism. However, this model may be incomplete, as other receptor profiles like 5-HT1A agonism also modulate dopamine release and could confer atypicality. Testing alternative sufficient mechanisms is crucial for developing novel APDs, especially for treatment-resistant schizophrenia where clozapine's unique efficacy remains incompletely understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacological profile combining strong D2 receptor blockade with potent 5-HT1A partial agonism, in the absence of significant 5-HT2A antagonism, is sufficient to produce antipsychotic-like efficacy in validated mouse models while minimizing catalepsy, a marker of EPS.
proposals.method: We will employ a selective pharmacological dissection in C57BL/6J mice using the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) model. A novel test compound with high D2 affinity (Ki < 5 nM) and high 5-HT1A partial agonist efficacy (Emax > 60% of 8-OH-DPAT), but minimal 5-HT2A activity (Ki > 1000 nM), will be synthesized or identified from existing libraries. Its effects will be compared to haloperidol (D2 antagonist), clozapine (multi-target atypical), and WAY-100635 (5-HT1A antagonist). Primary outcomes will be rescue of PCP-induced deficits in novel object recognition (memory) and prepulse inhibition (sensorimotor gating), assessed using established protocols. Catalepsy will be measured via the bar test. Receptor occupancy studies (ex vivo autoradiography) will confirm target engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly challenge the necessity of 5-HT2A antagonism for atypical APD profiles, providing proof-of-concept for a distinct mechanistic pathway. It will inform rational drug design by validating a 'D2/5-HT1A' signature as a viable strategy for achieving antipsychotic efficacy with a reduced EPS burden, potentially leading to new treatments.
proposals.novelty: While 5-HT1A agonism is recognized as a contributory factor in some APDs, its sufficiency as the primary atypicality mechanism alongside strong D2 blockade, explicitly in the absence of 5-HT2A blockade, has not been rigorously tested. This study deliberately isolates this receptor combination to test its standalone viability.

proposals.title: Transient Prolactin Elevation as a Novel Therapeutic Strategy: Rescue of Cognitive and Social Deficits in a Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Prolactin elevation is a well-known, often problematic side effect of many antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and amisulpride, and is a primary reason for clozapine's use in treatment-resistant cases due to its minimal prolactin impact. This has framed prolactin elevation as an exclusively adverse outcome. However, prolactin has complex neuromodulatory roles, influencing neuroplasticity, stress response, and social behavior—domains impaired in schizophrenia. The field lacks a systematic investigation into whether a controlled, transient increase in prolactin could have direct therapeutic benefits for core symptom domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a controlled, transient elevation of prolactin, administered independently of dopamine D2 receptor antagonism, will rescue cognitive deficits and negative symptoms (e.g., social withdrawal) in a validated mouse model of schizophrenia, without inducing hyperprolactinemia-associated side effects.
proposals.method: We will utilize the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, a well-established paradigm in our lab for inducing schizophrenia-like cognitive and social deficits. Mice will receive a transient prolactin elevation via subcutaneous injection of ovine prolactin (oPRL) at a low, pulsatile dosing regimen designed to mimic physiological peaks. Control groups will receive vehicle or chronic high-dose oPRL to model pathological hyperprolactinemia. Behavioral outcomes will be assessed using the Novel Object Recognition test for memory, the Social Interaction test for negative symptoms, and locomotor activity for positive symptom proxies. These will be benchmarked against the effects of clozapine and rapastinel from our prior work. Prolactin and corticosterone levels will be measured via ELISA to correlate behavioral effects with endocrine state.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct pre-clinical evidence for or against a therapeutic role of prolactin in schizophrenia-relevant behaviors. It will challenge the monolithic view of prolactin elevation as purely adverse and could identify a novel, hormone-based pathway for therapeutic intervention, particularly for cognitive and negative symptoms where current treatments are inadequate. The findings may also inform the side-effect profile management of existing antipsychotics.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the conventional narrative by investigating prolactin elevation as a potential mechanism for benefit, not just harm. It deliberately decouples prolactin's effects from dopamine D2 receptor blockade, a confounder in all prior clinical observations, to isolate its unique neuropsychiatric impact.

proposals.title: Quantitative Dissection of 5-HT1A Receptor Agonism in Mediating the Low Extrapyramidal Symptom Profile of Atypical Antipsychotics
proposals.motivation: Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS) remain a major limitation of antipsychotic treatment, contributing to non-adherence and poor outcomes. While atypical antipsychotics exhibit lower EPS, the precise pharmacological mechanisms are debated, with 5-HT1A receptor stimulation proposed as a key contributor. Isolating its specific effect from other receptor actions (e.g., 5-HT2A antagonism) is critical for rational drug design targeting improved tolerability in schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: Selective 5-HT1A receptor agonism, when combined with a D2 receptor antagonist, will dose-dependently reduce catalepsy and other EPS-like behaviors in mice, and this rescue will be blocked by a selective 5-HT1A antagonist, confirming a causal role.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established mouse model of EPS (haloperidol-induced catalepsy). Mice will receive co-administration of haloperidol with the selective 5-HT1A agonist (±)-8-OH-DPAT at varying doses. A parallel group will receive the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 prior to the agonist combination. Catalepsy will be quantified using the bar test. To extend findings, we will test the novel compound NU-1223, a 5-HT2cR agonist-like agent from our lab, with and without WAY-100635, to parse its 5-HT1A-mediated effects. Data will be analyzed using two-way ANOVA with post-hoc tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, quantitative evidence for the contribution of 5-HT1A stimulation to low EPS, moving beyond correlative clinical observations. It will establish a preclinical framework for isolating specific receptor contributions to complex behavioral profiles. The results will inform the development of next-generation antipsychotics with optimized receptor profiles for minimal motor side effects.
proposals.novelty: While 5-HT1A's role is hypothesized, this study directly isolates its contribution in a controlled D2 blockade model, using pharmacological dissection not commonly applied to this specific question. Furthermore, testing the mechanism in the context of our novel compound NU-1223 bridges the gap between a known mechanism and a new therapeutic candidate.

proposals.title: Rare Variant Burden Analysis of CNR1, LEP, and Related Genes in Phencyclidine-Induced Psychosis-Prone Mice
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model is a key translational tool for studying schizophrenia-like positive, negative, and cognitive symptoms. While common genetic variants contribute to schizophrenia risk, rare variants with large effect sizes may explain significant individual variation in susceptibility to drug-induced psychosis and treatment response, particularly for agents targeting systems like endocannabinoid (CNR1) and leptin (LEP) signaling implicated in metabolism and psychosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mice exhibiting extreme behavioral phenotypes (high vs. low susceptibility) in the subchronic PCP model will carry a differential burden of rare, likely deleterious, coding and regulatory variants in candidate genes CNR1 and LEP, and that these variants will modulate rescue by novel therapeutics like NU-1223 and rapastinel.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-exome sequencing on DNA from C57BL/6J mice stratified as 'PCP-susceptible' or 'PCP-resilient' based on a composite score of prepulse inhibition, novel object recognition, and social interaction deficits. Sequence data will be aligned to the mm10 genome using BWA-MEM, and variant calling performed with GATK best practices (pre-4.0). Rare variant burden (MAF<0.01) in CNR1, LEP, and a curated panel of interacting genes will be tested using SKAT-O. CRISPR/Cas9 will be used to introduce top candidate variants into naive mice for functional validation of PCP response and drug rescue.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify specific rare genetic variants that confer large effects on drug-induced psychosis susceptibility in a validated mouse model, moving beyond common variant polygenic risk. It will provide a mechanistic genetic explanation for individual differences in model fidelity and therapeutic response, informing the development of personalized preclinical strategies and highlighting novel druggable pathways.
proposals.novelty: While GWAS of behavioral traits in mice exist, a focused rare-variant analysis in the context of the well-characterized subchronic PCP model, linked to specific rescue compounds, is novel. It directly tests the 'missing heritability' hypothesis in a controlled environmental paradigm, bridging human genetics and preclinical psychopharmacology.

proposals.title: Validation of a Combined Genetic Risk Score for Antipsychotic Treatment Response in Pediatric Psychosis: A Multi-Cohort Replication Study
proposals.motivation: Early-onset psychosis is a severe condition where treatment response is highly variable, and identifying predictors is critical to improve outcomes. Pre-2026 research has identified preliminary genetic associations with antipsychotic efficacy in adults, but these findings lack robust validation in the distinct and vulnerable pediatric population. Replicating a genetic risk model in larger, independent youth cohorts is a necessary step towards potential clinical translation for personalized treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: A combined risk score based on three pre-specified genetic variants (e.g., in DRD2, HTR2C, or COMT genes, informed by prior adult studies) will significantly predict the trajectory of positive and negative symptom improvement in children and adolescents treated with atypical antipsychotics.
proposals.method: We will perform a retrospective genetic association study in two independent, pre-existing pediatric cohorts (total N > 800) treated with antipsychotics (e.g., risperidone, aripiprazole). The primary analysis will construct a polygenic risk score (PRS) by summing risk alleles from the three target variants, weighted by their previously reported effect sizes. This PRS will be tested as a predictor of symptom change (PANSS or SANS/SAPS scores) over 8-12 weeks using linear mixed-effects models, controlling for age, sex, baseline severity, and medication. Secondary analyses will examine PRS interaction with specific antipsychotics (e.g., clozapine vs. others).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale validation of a specific genetic model for antipsychotic response in pediatric psychosis, moving beyond single-variant associations. It will establish whether a simple, tractable genetic score has utility in predicting clinical outcomes in youth, informing future prospective trials of genetically-guided treatment selection.
proposals.novelty: While genetic studies in adult schizophrenia are common, focused replication of a defined risk model in dedicated pediatric cohorts is rare. The approach bridges Dr. Meltzer's expertise in novel therapeutics (e.g., 5-HT2cR agonists) and treatment-resistant illness by seeking biomarkers that could identify subpopulations for specific interventions early in the disease course.

proposals.title: Refining the Schizophrenia Diagnostic Signature: A Machine Learning Approach to Differentiate Schizophrenia from Bipolar Disorder and Major Depression Using Clinical and Neurocognitive Data
proposals.motivation: Traditional case-control studies comparing schizophrenia (SZ) to healthy controls identify biomarkers that may reflect general psychopathology rather than SZ-specific pathology. This limits their utility for differential diagnosis, a critical clinical challenge, especially given symptom overlap with disorders like bipolar disorder (BD) and major depressive disorder (MDD). Refining diagnostic boundaries is essential for developing more targeted treatments and improving patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained to distinguish individuals with schizophrenia from those with other major psychiatric disorders (BD, MDD) will identify a more specific neurocognitive and clinical signature of schizophrenia than models trained against healthy controls, and this refined signature will improve predictive accuracy for SZ in independent validation cohorts.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing, pre-2026 clinical datasets (e.g., from the NIMH Repository) containing detailed symptom ratings (PANSS, SANS, YMRS), neurocognitive battery scores, and demographic data for patients with SZ, BD, and MDD. Using a supervised learning framework, we will train and compare two Random Forest classifiers: one predicting SZ vs. healthy controls (HC), and another predicting SZ vs. BD/MDD. We will employ feature importance analysis (Gini impurity) from the SZ vs. BD/MDD model to identify the most discriminative variables. Model performance will be validated via nested cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond generic case-control contrasts to identify a more precise, clinically actionable profile of schizophrenia. It will provide a data-driven method to aid in differential diagnosis and could inform the development of more specific biomarkers for clinical trials, particularly for treatments targeting cognitive or negative symptoms where diagnostic confusion is common.
proposals.novelty: Instead of seeking a 'disease signal' against health, we explicitly model the 'boundary signal' between schizophrenia and its closest diagnostic neighbors, inverting the standard case-control paradigm to address a core problem in psychiatric nosology.

proposals.title: Dissociating Serotonergic from Dopaminergic Mechanisms in Atypical Antipsychotic Efficacy: A Preclinical Study Using Selective Receptor Antagonists in the Phencyclidine Model
proposals.motivation: The defining feature of atypical antipsychotics (AAPDs) is their combined antagonism of dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, a mechanism widely considered essential for their improved efficacy on negative/cognitive symptoms and lower extrapyramidal side effect profile. However, the precise contribution of serotonergic modulation to the full therapeutic benefit of AAPDs remains ambiguous, complicating the rational design of novel agents. Clarifying this is critical for developing more targeted, potentially simpler, and better-tolerated pharmacotherapies for schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while 5-HT2A/C receptor antagonism contributes to the rescue of certain cognitive and negative symptom domains in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, a significant portion of the behavioral efficacy of prototypical AAPDs can be achieved through optimized D2 receptor modulation combined with other non-serotonergic pathways (e.g., glutamatergic or muscarinic).
proposals.method: We will employ a selective receptor blockade strategy in the well-validated subchronic PCP mouse model of schizophrenia. Mice will be treated with clozapine or risperidone, co-administered with selective 5-HT2A (e.g., M100907) or 5-HT2C (e.g., SB-242084) receptor antagonists to pharmacologically 'strip away' their serotonergic action. Behavioral outcomes will be assessed using a comprehensive battery: novel object recognition (memory), social interaction (negative symptoms), and locomotor hyperactivity (positive symptoms). Parallel experiments will test a D2/5-HT2A 'silent' agent (like amisulpride) and a D2 partial agonist (like aripiprazole) as comparators to isolate dopaminergic mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, mechanistic dissection of the serotonergic component in AAPD action, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will identify which symptom domains are most dependent on 5-HT2 receptor modulation, informing future drug discovery. The results could challenge the dogma that combined D2/5-HT2A antagonism is a necessary condition for an 'atypical' profile, potentially validating alternative pathways.
proposals.novelty: While the role of 5-HT2A in AAPD action is established, systematically testing its *necessity* across the full symptom spectrum using selective pharmacological subtraction in a translational model is novel. This approach directly tests the core assumption of the serotonin-dopamine hypothesis of AAPDs, leveraging Dr. Meltzer's expertise with the PCP model and novel compounds like NU-1223.

proposals.title: Differential Effects of Atypical Antipsychotics on Cognitive Domains in a Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model: A Comparative Study of Clozapine, Aripiprazole, and NU-1223
proposals.motivation: Atypical antipsychotics are often presumed to offer a uniform cognitive benefit over typicals, a key rationale for their widespread use. However, this 'class effect' assumption is clinically significant yet poorly validated, as individual drugs have distinct receptor profiles (e.g., 5-HT2cR agonism, partial D2 agonism). Pre-2026 work, including Meltzer's on NU-1223 and rapastinel, suggests cognition can be rescued via diverse mechanisms, challenging the uniformity hypothesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cognitive-enhancing effects of atypical antipsychotics are not a uniform class effect but vary significantly between specific agents, correlating with their unique pharmacodynamic profiles rather than their shared atypical classification.
proposals.method: We will employ the established subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model of schizophrenia-like cognitive deficits. Following a 7-day PCP regimen, mice will be treated chronically (14 days) with equipotent doses of clozapine (broad antagonist), aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist), and the simplified alstonine analog NU-1223 (5-HT2cR agonist-like). Cognitive domains (working memory, novel object recognition, cognitive flexibility) will be assessed using the T-maze, novel object recognition test, and attentional set-shifting task, respectively.
