Data will be analyzed using mixed-design ANOVAs with post-hoc comparisons to detect drug-specific cognitive profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, head-to-head comparison of cognitive outcomes for mechanistically distinct atypicals in a validated model, moving beyond class-level generalizations. It will clarify whether specific receptor actions (e.g., 5-HT2cR agonism) confer unique cognitive advantages, informing more precise, mechanism-based drug selection for cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tests single drugs against PCP deficits, a systematic comparison of multiple atypicals with differing primary mechanisms (including the novel candidate NU-1223) to challenge the 'class effect' paradigm is novel. It leverages Meltzer's recent compounds to probe the heterogeneity within the atypical class itself.

proposals.title: Design and Preclinical Characterization of Novel 5-HT2C Receptor Agonists with Minimal D2 Antagonism for the Treatment of Schizophrenia without Metabolic Side Effects
proposals.motivation: Current atypical antipsychotics, while effective for positive and negative symptoms, frequently cause severe metabolic side effects like weight gain and insulin resistance, leading to poor adherence and increased cardiovascular risk. This is a major clinical problem, particularly for long-term treatment. The need for agents that separate antipsychotic efficacy from metabolic disturbance is urgent and unmet.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel compounds with potent 5-HT2C receptor agonist activity and minimal D2 receptor antagonism will rescue schizophrenia-like deficits in validated mouse models while avoiding the metabolic dysregulation associated with strong D2/H1/M1 antagonism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of novel compounds based on the simplified alstonine analog scaffold (e.g., NU-1223), optimizing for 5-HT2C agonist potency and selectivity over 5-HT2A/B. High-throughput in vitro screening will profile affinity/functionality at D2, H1, M1, and 5-HT2 family receptors. Lead compounds will be tested in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, assessing rescue of memory deficits (as in rapastinel studies) and PCP-induced hyperlocomotion. Parallel longitudinal studies in mice will measure body weight, food intake, and glucose tolerance to evaluate metabolic impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify and characterize a new class of potential antipsychotic agents designed from first principles to avoid metabolic side effects. It will provide critical preclinical proof-of-concept for a 5-HT2C-agonist-centric mechanism as a viable path for drug development. The findings could redefine the pharmacological strategy for next-generation antipsychotics.
proposals.novelty: While 5-HT2C agonism is known to suppress appetite, its integration as the primary efficacy driver in a novel antipsychotic scaffold, deliberately minimizing D2 blockade, is a distinct and non-obvious strategy compared to existing atypical antipsychotics which rely on combined D2/5-HT2A antagonism.

proposals.title: Development of a Precision Medicine Algorithm for First-Line Atypical Antipsychotic Selection in Schizophrenia Based on Individual Symptom and Risk Profiles
proposals.motivation: Current first-line treatment with atypical antipsychotics for schizophrenia remains a trial-and-error process, leading to prolonged periods of inadequate symptom control, poor tolerability, and increased risk of relapse. Pre-2026, while clozapine guidelines exist for treatment resistance, there is a critical lack of evidence-based, data-driven protocols for selecting the optimal first-line atypical agent (e.g., risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole) based on a patient's initial presentation of positive, negative, and cognitive symptoms, and individual risk factors for metabolic, extrapyramidal, or cardiovascular side effects.
proposals.hypothesis: A multivariate algorithm, integrating baseline symptom dimensions and individual risk profiles, can predict the atypical antipsychotic with the highest probability of achieving efficacy and tolerability, thereby optimizing first-line treatment outcomes compared to current standard-of-care selection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, randomized, pragmatic trial. Newly diagnosed or currently untreated schizophrenia patients (n=450) will be comprehensively assessed at baseline for symptom clusters (PANSS, cognitive battery) and risk profiles (BMI, family history of diabetes, etc.). Patients will be randomized to either a Treatment-as-Usual (TAU) arm, where the clinician freely chooses an atypical, or an Algorithm arm. The algorithm, developed using pre-2026 data and a Bayesian decision framework, will recommend one of three first-line atypicals based on the baseline assessment. The primary outcome is the rate of treatment success (adequate response without intolerable side effects) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes include time to discontinuation and specific symptom domain improvements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorously tested, clinical-trial-validated algorithm for personalized first-line antipsychotic selection. It will move the field beyond class-level efficacy comparisons toward precision prescribing, potentially reducing the time to effective treatment and the morbidity associated with failed trials. The resulting protocol could be integrated into electronic health records to guide clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Meltzer and others has focused on novel agents (e.g., NU-1223, rapastinel) or clozapine's unique role, this proposal uniquely applies a precision medicine framework to the common, high-stakes clinical decision of first-line atypical selection, using a pragmatic trial design to test a predictive algorithm in real-world conditions.

proposals.title: Computational Repurposing of FDA-Approved Drugs for Atypical Antipsychotic Activity: A Multi-Target Pharmacology and Phenotypic Screening Approach
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of atypical antipsychotics like clozapine, treatment-resistant schizophrenia remains a major clinical challenge, and many patients suffer from debilitating side effects. Computational drug repurposing offers a rapid, cost-effective strategy to identify new therapeutic candidates from existing drug libraries, potentially uncovering novel multi-target mechanisms that define the 'atypical' profile of improved efficacy and tolerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational screening pipeline integrating multi-target receptor affinity prediction and phenotypic signature matching can identify FDA-approved drugs with a pharmacological profile predictive of atypical antipsychotic efficacy, which can then be validated in established preclinical models of psychosis.
proposals.method: We will first curate a reference pharmacological signature for atypical antipsychotics (e.g., high 5-HT2A/D2 affinity ratio, 5-HT1A/5-HT2C agonism) using ChEMBL and PDSP Ki databases. Using this signature, we will screen the DrugBank library via a two-tiered computational approach: 1) structure-based virtual screening with molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) against key receptor targets (D2, 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, 5-HT1A), and 2) signature-based similarity analysis using the Connectivity Map (CMap) to match gene expression profiles induced by known atypicals. Top-ranked candidates will be prioritized for in vitro binding assays and subsequent testing in the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model, assessing prepulse inhibition, social interaction, and cognitive deficits.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, ranked list of repurposing candidates with predicted atypical antipsychotic properties, providing new leads for the treatment of schizophrenia, particularly for cognitive and negative symptoms. It will also establish and validate a computational framework for identifying multi-target CNS drugs, moving beyond single-target screening paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While computational screening is established, its application specifically to define and discover the complex, polypharmacological 'atypical' profile is novel. This approach directly bridges the gap between Meltzer's work on multi-target agents (e.g., NU-1223) and modern bioinformatics, offering a systematic method to exploit existing chemical space for new psychiatric therapeutics.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Resolution of 5-HT2C Receptor Agonist-Mediated Inhibition of Striatal Dopamine Neuron Hyperactivity in a Mouse Model of Extrapyramidal Symptoms
proposals.motivation: Atypical antipsychotics with serotonin 5-HT2C receptor (5-HT2CR) agonist-like properties, such as clozapine and the experimental compound NU-1223, show reduced extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS). Pre-2026 evidence suggests this is due to serotonergic modulation of midbrain dopaminergic pathways, but the precise neuron- and circuit-level mechanisms in the striatum, the key site for EPS pathophysiology, remain poorly mapped. Understanding this microcircuitry is critical for developing novel, EPS-free antipsychotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 5-HT2CR agonism, via compounds like NU-1223, rescues phencyclidine-induced locomotor deficits and catalepsy by selectively inhibiting a hyperactive subpopulation of substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) dopamine neurons projecting to the dorsolateral striatum, and that this inhibition is mediated through enhanced GABAergic feedforward inhibition from the ventral tegmental area.
proposals.method: We will use a combination of in vivo fiber photometry and single-cell calcium imaging in transgenic TH-Cre mice to record activity from SNc dopamine neurons projecting to the dorsolateral striatum during a catalepsy test, following subchronic phencyclidine treatment and subsequent NU-1223 administration. Circuit mapping will be achieved using channelrhodopsin-assisted circuit mapping (CRACM) in ex vivo brain slices from these mice, combined with viral tracing and cell-type-specific Cre-driver lines to identify and manipulate the specific GABAergic interneurons in the VTA that are activated by 5-HT2CR stimulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, single-cell evidence linking 5-HT2CR agonist action to the normalization of a specific, pathology-relevant dopaminergic subcircuit, directly connecting a molecular mechanism to a behavioral rescue of EPS-like symptoms. It will identify a precise neural circuit target (e.g., VTA GABA→SNc DA) for future drug development aimed at minimizing motor side effects.
proposals.novelty: While the behavioral profile of 5-HT2CR agonists is known, applying single-cell imaging and optogenetic circuit dissection specifically to the EPS-relevant dorsolateral striatum pathway during antipsychotic rescue represents a novel, mechanistic down-scaling of Dr. Meltzer's prior behavioral and neurochemical work with NU-1223 and phencyclidine.

proposals.title: Comparative Translational Validity of Etiological Models for Schizophrenia: Phencyclidine vs. Maternal Immune Activation on Cognitive and Symptom Domains in Mice
proposals.motivation: The sub-chronic phencyclidine (PCP) NMDAR antagonism model is the dominant cognitive impairment in schizophrenia (CIS) model, but its exclusive focus on NMDAR hypofunction may not capture the full etiological complexity of the disorder. Pre-2026, alternative models like maternal immune activation (MIA) are gaining traction for their link to genetic and environmental risk factors, yet a direct, systematic comparison of their translational cognitive and symptomatic profiles is lacking. Determining which model produces deficits most analogous to the treatment-resistant cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia patients is critical for developing more effective pharmacotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the MIA model will produce a more translationally relevant and pharmacologically distinct profile of cognitive deficits and negative symptom analogs compared to the sub-chronic PCP model, which may be more specific to NMDAR dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will employ a between-subjects design in C57BL/6J mice using three groups: 1) sub-chronic PCP (10 mg/kg/day for 7 days), 2) MIA (poly(I:C) gestational exposure), and 3) vehicle controls. Cognitive domains (working memory, cognitive flexibility) will be assessed using the novel object recognition and attentional set-shifting tasks. Negative symptom analogs will be measured via social interaction and sucrose preference tests. Following behavioral characterization, we will test the efficacy of two mechanistically distinct pro-cognitive agents: rapastinel (a glycine-site NMDAR modulator, known to rescue PCP deficits) and NU-1223 (a 5-HT2cR agonist-like compound), to determine model-specific pharmacological responsiveness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, empirical comparison of two leading etiological models, clarifying which produces cognitive deficits more resistant to known rescue agents and thus potentially more analogous to treatment-resistant schizophrenia. It will inform future preclinical drug discovery by identifying the most translationally valid model for screening novel compounds targeting cognitive and negative symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While both models are studied independently, a head-to-head comparison of their cognitive and symptomatic profiles, followed by challenge with both an NMDAR-targeting agent and a non-NMDAR serotonergic agent, is novel. This moves beyond validating a single model to critically evaluating which etiological pathway yields deficits most relevant for drug development.

proposals.title: Causal Pathway Stratification in Early Psychosis: A Precision Medicine Trial Targeting Cognition-Psychosis Dynamics for Functional Recovery
proposals.motivation: Functional recovery in schizophrenia remains poor, partly due to heterogeneous relationships between cognitive deficits and psychotic symptoms. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Meltzer's work with PCP models and novel compounds like NU-1223 and rapastinel, highlights distinct pharmacological targets for cognitive and psychotic domains, but clinical translation lacks a method to match treatment to an individual's predominant causal pathology. This gap limits the efficacy of both cognitive and antipsychotic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stratifying individuals with early psychosis based on empirically derived, individual-level causal pathways between cognition and symptoms, and then assigning a pathway-matched intervention, will yield superior functional outcomes compared to non-matched treatment.
proposals.method: Phase 1: 100 early psychosis patients undergo 12 weeks of intensive longitudinal monitoring using smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for symptoms and digital cognitive sampling (e.g., MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery brief forms). Individual dynamic Bayesian network models will estimate person-specific Granger-causal or transfer entropy relationships to stratify participants into 'cognition-leading', 'psychosis-leading', or 'coupled' subgroups. Phase 2: A 24-week double-blind, randomized controlled trial where each subgroup is randomized to receive either a matched intervention (cognitive remediation for cognition-leading; antipsychotic optimization with a D2/5-HT2A antagonist like lurasidone for psychosis-leading; combined for coupled) or a non-matched control. The primary outcome is change in the UPSA-B functional capacity score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence for a causal-pathway-driven precision psychiatry framework in psychosis. It will generate a novel, data-driven stratification biomarker and directly test whether aligning treatment with individualized pathology improves functional recovery, a critical unmet need.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond static symptom clusters or cognitive profiles to model *individual temporal causality* as a stratification tool is a paradigm shift. Pre-2026 work has not attempted to use such dynamic models to prospectively assign and test domain-specific interventions in a clinical trial.

proposals.title: Targeting Cognitive Impairment with the Glycine Modulator Rapastinel to Improve Functional Outcome in Schizophrenia: A Randomized, Controlled Trial in Patients with Stable Psychosis
proposals.motivation: Cognitive impairment is a core feature of schizophrenia and a primary determinant of poor functional outcome, yet no approved treatments exist. Current antipsychotics primarily target positive symptoms, with limited impact on cognition. This study directly tests whether improving cognition, independent of further psychosis reduction, can enhance real-world functioning, addressing a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with schizophrenia whose psychosis is adequately controlled, adjunctive treatment with the NMDA receptor glycine-site modulator rapastinel, which has demonstrated pro-cognitive effects in preclinical models, will significantly improve cognitive performance and, consequently, functional outcome, without further reducing positive symptom severity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-week, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. 120 clinically stable outpatients with schizophrenia (PANSS positive subscale ≤ 18, on stable atypical antipsychotic regimen) will be randomized to adjunctive rapastinel (450 mg IV weekly, per pre-2026 phase II protocols) or placebo. Primary outcomes are change in the MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery (MCCB) composite score and the UCSD Performance-Based Skills Assessment (UPSA-B). Secondary outcomes include symptom scales and functional capacity measures. Analysis will use mixed-model repeated measures (MMRM) to assess treatment effects on cognition and functioning, with mediation analysis to test if cognitive change drives functional improvement.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct clinical test of the hypothesis that cognitive enhancement, decoupled from antipsychotic effect, improves functional outcome. It will generate crucial evidence for a novel treatment paradigm in schizophrenia and clarify the role of NMDA receptor modulation as a pro-cognitive strategy in stable patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have tested cognitive enhancers, they often involved patients with active symptoms, conflating cognitive and antipsychotic effects. This proposal uniquely targets a stable population to isolate the functional impact of cognitive improvement, leveraging rapastinel's distinct, prolonged mechanism shown in Meltzer's preclinical work.

proposals.title: Investigating the Neurodevelopmental and Inflammatory Origins of Glutamatergic/GABAergic Deficits and Cognitive Dysfunction in a Prenatal Immune Activation Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Current treatments for schizophrenia, including atypical antipsychotics and novel agents like rapastinel, primarily target downstream neurotransmitter dysfunction, offering limited efficacy for cognitive deficits. Pre-2026 evidence strongly implicates upstream glutamatergic and GABAergic pathology in cognitive impairment, but the primary etiological drivers—whether neurodevelopmental disruption, neuroinflammation, or their interaction—remain unclear. Elucidating these primary insults is critical for developing preventative or disease-modifying interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cognitive deficits in schizophrenia arise from neurodevelopmental and inflammatory insults that precede and drive the secondary glutamatergic/GABAergic dysfunction observed in established illness. Specifically, we predict that a prenatal immune activation (PIA) model will induce persistent microglial activation and parvalbumin interneuron deficits, which correlate with and predict later NMDA receptor hypofunction and cognitive impairment.
proposals.method: We will use a maternal immune activation (MIA) mouse model (poly(I:C) injection at gestational day 12.5) to induce a neurodevelopmental insult. Offspring will be assessed longitudinally. In adolescence and early adulthood, we will quantify microglial activation (Iba1 immunohistochemistry, cytokine profiling) and parvalbumin interneuron density in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Concurrently, we will assess NMDA receptor function via ex vivo electrophysiology and evaluate cognitive performance using the novel object recognition and Y-maze tests, tasks previously used in our lab with phencyclidine models. Statistical analyses will employ correlation and mediation models to test if inflammatory/neurodevelopmental markers mediate the link between MIA and later glutamatergic/cognitive deficits.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the causal primacy of neurodevelopmental/inflammatory mechanisms over neurotransmitter dysfunction in schizophrenia-related cognitive deficits. It will provide a mechanistic framework linking prenatal insult to the circuit-level pathology observed in patients. Findings could redirect therapeutic strategies toward early immunomodulatory or neuroprotective targets, moving beyond symptomatic rescue of neurotransmitter systems.
proposals.novelty: While PIA models exist, this proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal assessment of inflammation, specific interneuron populations, and NMDA function within the same model to establish mediation, directly testing the 'primary insult' hypothesis. It applies the mechanistic dissection approach used in our lab's psychotomimetic drug models to a neurodevelopmental etiology with greater translational relevance to disease origins.

proposals.title: Circuit-Breaker Analysis of Novel Object Recognition Deficits in Schizophrenia-Relevant Transgenic Mice: Mapping Gene-to-Circuit Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits, including impaired novel object recognition (NOR), are core features of schizophrenia but remain poorly treated by current antipsychotics. While genetic risk factors (e.g., DISC1, NRG1) and neural circuits (e.g., perirhinal-hippocampal-prefrontal) are implicated, the causal pathway from specific genetic lesions to specific circuit failures is unknown, hindering targeted intervention. A systematic, causal mapping approach is needed to isolate the neural 'faults' underlying this key cognitive symptom.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct schizophrenia-relevant genetic mutations disrupt NOR by causing dysfunction in specific, non-overlapping nodes of the canonical NOR circuit, and that chemogenetic rescue of activity in the affected node will be sufficient to restore normal behavior in a mutation-specific manner.
proposals.method: We will first establish a detailed functional map of the NOR circuit in wild-type mice using activity-dependent markers (c-Fos) and pathway-specific chemogenetic (DREADD) activation/inhibition. We will then employ this map in transgenic mouse models (e.g., DISC1, NRG1, 22q11.2 deletion models) exhibiting NOR deficits. Using a 'circuit-breaker' strategy, we will perform cell-type-specific, reversible DREADD-mediated silencing of candidate nodes (e.g., perirhinal cortex, ventral hippocampus, medial prefrontal cortex) in mutant mice to test if a given genetic lesion requires disruption of a specific circuit component to produce the NOR deficit, and conversely, if chemogenetic activation of that node can rescue it.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, circuit-level explanation for how specific schizophrenia-risk genes produce a measurable cognitive deficit. It will move beyond correlation to identify precise, mutation-specific neural targets for remediation. The findings could inform the development of circuit-based, personalized interventions for cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting a fault-isolation engineering principle to systematically dissect gene-to-circuit relationships in psychiatric disease models, and in testing the mutation-specificity of circuit dysfunction, which is not addressed by standard lesion or pharmacological studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Hit Model of Progressive Cognitive Impairment in Mice: Synergistic Effects of Chronic Anticholinergic Challenge and Systemic Inflammation on Cognition and Neurophysiology
proposals.motivation: Current animal models of cognitive impairment, particularly for schizophrenia, often rely on single acute insults (e.g., phencyclidine) that incompletely capture the progressive, multi-factorial etiology seen in patients. There is a critical need for more translationally robust models that combine chronic, low-level vulnerabilities with secondary insults to better mimic the complex pathophysiology and test novel remediation strategies like those targeting 5-HT2cR or NMDA receptor function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, low-dose anticholinergic exposure will prime the brain, creating a vulnerable state where a secondary, sub-threshold neuroinflammatory insult will synergistically induce progressive, domain-specific cognitive deficits and neurophysiological alterations that are more severe and persistent than those caused by either insult alone.
proposals.method: C57BL/6J mice will receive chronic, low-dose scopolamine in drinking water for 8 weeks. At week 4, a subset will receive a secondary hit of low-dose lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to induce systemic inflammation. Longitudinal assessment will include a cognitive battery (novel object recognition, attentional set-shifting, and spatial working memory in the T-maze) and in-vivo biomarkers: resting-state EEG for neurophysiology and plasma cytokine levels (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) for peripheral inflammation. In a subsequent phase, the model will be used to test rescue by compounds like rapastinel (GLYX-13) or NU-1223.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, etiologically plausible preclinical model with high face and construct validity for progressive cognitive impairment. It will identify convergent pathophysiological mechanisms beyond isolated cholinergic dysfunction and provide a robust platform for evaluating remediation strategies with broader mechanistic targets, thereby increasing predictive validity for complex disorders like schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential, chronic 'multi-hit' design, moving beyond acute single-insult models to probe synergistic interactions between a cholinergic vulnerability and a metabolic/ inflammatory challenge, and the longitudinal integration of behavioral, neurophysiological, and inflammatory biomarkers to characterize progression.

proposals.title: Enhancing Cognitive Network Resilience: Remediating Scopolamine-Induced Novel Object Recognition Deficits via Environmental Enrichment and BDNF Modulation
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, particularly in memory, are core drivers of functional disability and remain inadequately treated by current antipsychotics. The scopolamine-induced deficit in the novel object recognition (NOR) task is a well-validated translational model of cholinergic dysfunction underlying these impairments. However, current remediation strategies in models often focus on direct receptor-level reversal, mirroring a symptom-suppression approach rather than promoting enduring cognitive resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scopolamine-induced NOR deficits represent a state of fragile cognitive network connectivity, and that interventions aimed at enhancing systemic neural resilience—specifically environmental enrichment and adjunctive BDNF-promoting agents—will not only rescue the deficit but also confer protection against subsequent cholinergic challenge, shifting the network's pharmacological 'tipping point'.
proposals.method: We will first establish a dose-response curve for scopolamine in C57BL/6 mice to define the precise 'tipping point' dose for NOR deficit. Mice will then be treated with scopolamine at this dose and randomized to receive: 1) standard housing, 2) environmental enrichment (EE), 3) systemic administration of the TrkB agonist 7,8-Dihydroxyflavone (7,8-DHF), or 4) EE+7,8-DHF. NOR will be assessed post-treatment. A subset of 'rescued' mice will be rechallenged with a sub-threshold scopolamine dose to test for sustained resilience. Ex vivo analysis of hippocampal BDNF/TrkB signaling and markers of synaptic plasticity (e.g., PSD-95, synaptophysin) will correlate with behavioral outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for cognitive remediation, moving beyond receptor-specific reversal to a systems-level resilience model. It will generate pre-clinical evidence for non-pharmacological (EE) and adjunctive pharmacological strategies to build cognitive reserve, directly informing the development of next-generation pro-cognitive therapies for schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: The application of ecological resilience theory to a cognitive pharmacology model is novel, as is the explicit test of whether a rescued network shows increased robustness to secondary challenge, a key tenet of resilience not typically assessed in standard reversal paradigms.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Characterization and Pharmacological Rescue of Scopolamine-Induced Novel Object Recognition Deficits: Relevance to Cognitive Impairment in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, particularly in memory and attention, are a major determinant of functional disability and remain inadequately treated by current antipsychotics. The scopolamine-induced deficit in the novel object recognition (NOR) task is a widely used, rapid preclinical model of cognitive impairment, but its neurochemical and circuit-level mechanisms are incompletely understood. A detailed characterization of this model is crucial for validating its translational relevance and for identifying novel therapeutic targets beyond dopamine and serotonin systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scopolamine-induced NOR deficits result from a specific disruption of muscarinic receptor-mediated signaling in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus, and that these deficits can be selectively rescued by pharmacological agents acting on downstream targets implicated in synaptic plasticity, such as NMDA receptor positive allosteric modulators (PAMs) or mTOR pathway modulators.
proposals.method: We will first establish dose-response and time-course profiles for scopolamine-induced NOR deficits in C57BL/6 mice. Using this optimized model, we will employ in vivo microdialysis in freely moving mice to measure concurrent changes in acetylcholine, glutamate, and dopamine release in the medial prefrontal cortex and hippocampus during the NOR task. We will then test the rescue efficacy of pre-identified compounds: the NMDA receptor PAM rapastinel (based on Meltzer's prior PCP model work), the mTOR inhibitor rapamycin, and the 5-HT2C receptor agonist-like compound NU-1223. Behavioral rescue will be correlated with ex vivo analyses of synaptic protein markers (e.g., PSD-95, GluA1) and mTOR pathway activation (pS6) in relevant brain regions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a comprehensive neurochemical and mechanistic profile of a key preclinical model of cognitive impairment, directly linking cholinergic antagonism to altered glutamate dynamics and synaptic plasticity markers. It will validate or challenge the translational utility of the scopolamine-NOR model for schizophrenia drug discovery by testing rescue with mechanistically distinct compounds. The findings could identify novel combination treatment strategies targeting cholinergic-glutamatergic interactions for cognitive symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While the scopolamine-NOR model is common, a detailed in vivo neurochemical characterization during task performance, coupled with testing rescue by non-D2-based mechanisms (specifically rapastinel and NU-1223) emerging from Meltzer's own research program, is novel. This directly bridges his recent work in PCP models to a cholinergic model, testing mechanism-transcending efficacy.

proposals.title: Activity-Dependent, Positive Allosteric Modulation of GABA-A Receptors Rescues Cognitive Deficits in a Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: GABAergic hypofunction is a core pathophysiological feature of schizophrenia, contributing to cortical excitation-inhibition (E/I) imbalance and cognitive deficits like impaired novel object recognition (NOR). Current GABAergic drugs, such as benzodiazepines, produce non-selective, tonic enhancement leading to sedation and tolerance, failing to correct the dynamic, circuit-specific dysfunction. A pharmacological strategy that adaptively modulates GABAergic transmission based on local neural activity could precisely restore E/I balance and cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel, activity-dependent positive allosteric modulators (AD-PAMs) of GABA-A receptors, which selectively enhance GABAergic transmission in hyperactive circuits, will rescue NOR deficits in the subchronic phencyclidine (scPCP) mouse model by normalizing E/I balance in a targeted, feedback-dependent manner.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel AD-PAM candidates designed with a voltage-sensing moiety and a GABA-A receptor PAM domain, conferring use-dependence. In scPCP-treated C57BL/6 mice, we will first assess lead compounds on NOR performance. Concurrently, we will use in vivo electrophysiology (chronic multi-unit recordings in medial prefrontal cortex and hippocampus) to measure baseline hyperactivity and test the compound's ability to normalize firing rates specifically during object exploration. Dose-response curves and comparisons to standard PAMs (e.g., diazepam) and the reference compound rapastinel will establish specificity and efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel class of 'smart' GABAergic modulators that act as neural circuit tuners, moving beyond static receptor blockade or activation. It will directly test a causal link between activity-dependent GABAergic modulation and the rescue of a specific cognitive endophenotype of schizophrenia, offering a new therapeutic strategy for cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While existing PAMs produce blanket inhibition, our approach is novel in its explicit design for activity-dependence, analogous to adaptive signal processing, allowing it to selectively correct pathological hyperactivity without suppressing normal neural function, a key limitation of current GABAergic pharmacology.

proposals.title: A Multi-Domain Cognitive Battery in the Subchronic Phencyclidine Mouse Model: Evaluating the Effects of 5-HT2cR Agonist-like Compounds on Memory, Attention, and Cognitive Flexibility
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, particularly in domains like visual recognition memory, attention, and cognitive flexibility, are core drivers of functional disability and are poorly treated by current antipsychotics. Pre-2026, the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model is widely used, but cognitive assessment is often limited to single-domain tests like the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) task, failing to capture the multidimensional nature of cognitive impairment. A comprehensive, validated cognitive battery is needed to robustly evaluate novel pharmacotherapies, such as 5-HT2cR-targeting compounds, which show promise in preclinical studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the NOR test with complementary assays of attention (5-Choice Serial Reaction Time Task, 5-CSRTT) and cognitive flexibility (Attentional Set-Shifting Task, ASST) will provide a more sensitive and pharmacologically discriminating profile of cognitive rescue by NU-1223 and rapastinel in the subchronic PCP mouse model than NOR alone.
proposals.method: C57BL/6J mice will receive subchronic PCP (or vehicle) followed by a washout period. Mice will then be trained and tested sequentially on a standardized cognitive battery: NOR for visual recognition memory, the 5-CSRTT for sustained and selective attention, and the ASST for extra-dimensional set-shifting ability. Separate cohorts will receive acute or repeated administration of NU-1223, rapastinel, or vehicle prior to testing each domain. Performance metrics (discrimination index, accuracy, omissions, perseverative errors) will be analyzed using mixed-design ANOVAs to compare drug effects across domains and against vehicle/control groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a practical, multi-domain cognitive testing protocol for the PCP model, enhancing its translational validity. It will determine whether the promising pro-cognitive effects of NU-1223 and rapastinel extend beyond memory to other treatment-resistant cognitive domains, providing a stronger preclinical rationale for their development. The data will clarify if these compounds have broad or domain-specific cognitive benefits.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Meltzer's lab has extensively used NOR to evaluate novel compounds, systematically combining it with operant-based tests of attention and cognitive flexibility in a single PCP cohort to generate a compound-specific cognitive profile is novel. This approach moves beyond confirming a memory rescue to mapping a comprehensive cognitive efficacy signature.

proposals.title: Validating Neurodevelopmental Insult Models in Rodents via Human Biomarker Trajectories: A Causal Pipeline from Inflammation to Glutamatergic/GABAergic Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Current rodent models of schizophrenia, like the subchronic phencyclidine (PCP) model used in our lab, primarily capture downstream glutamatergic dysfunction and cognitive deficits. However, the upstream neurodevelopmental or inflammatory insults hypothesized to initiate the human disease process are not systematically validated against human biomarker data. This gap limits the translational relevance of these models for understanding early pathophysiology and for developing preventive interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rodent models of early-life neurodevelopmental insults (e.g., maternal immune activation, neonatal ventral hippocampus lesion) will sequentially reproduce the proteomic/metabolomic biomarker trajectory observed in human clinical high-risk and first-episode psychosis cohorts, specifically tracing a causal path from inflammatory markers to subsequent glutamatergic and GABAergic system deficits.
proposals.method: We will first establish a human reference biomarker trajectory by analyzing longitudinal plasma/serum proteomic (e.g., inflammatory cytokines, BDNF) and metabolomic data from pre-2026 CHR and FEP studies. Parallelly, we will subject rodents to distinct early-life insults and collect serial blood and brain tissue (prefrontal cortex, hippocampus) at developmental timepoints corresponding to the human stages. Biomarker profiles will be analyzed using multivariate partial least squares-discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) to identify stage-specific signatures. The degree of alignment between human and rodent trajectories will be quantitatively assessed using similarity network fusion (SNF) algorithms, and correlated with behavioral outcomes in novel object recognition (NOR) and other cognitive tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will create a novel, biomarker-driven validation pipeline for etiologically relevant rodent models, moving beyond behavioral phenocopy. It will directly bridge human biomarker discovery with causal neurodevelopmental mechanisms, identifying which preclinical insults best recapitulate the human pathophysiological cascade. This will provide a more precise platform for testing stage-specific interventions, such as rapastinel or 5-HT2cR agonists, aimed at halting progression.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in using longitudinal human biomarker signatures as a quantitative template to validate the *causal progression* in rodent models, rather than just matching end-state phenotypes. This approach of 'trajectory validation' is distinct from existing work that correlates single timepoint biomarkers with behavior.

proposals.title: Cross-Species Validation of the Phencyclidine-Induced Novel Object Recognition Deficit Model Using Proteomic Biomarkers from Clinical High-Risk for Psychosis Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The phencyclidine (PCP) mouse model is widely used to study cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, particularly via the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) test, but its translational validity for the human disease state remains debated. Concurrently, human studies of Clinical High-Risk (CHR) or first-episode psychosis cohorts have identified preliminary proteomic and metabolomic biomarker signatures, yet these lack direct linkage to established preclinical models. Bridging this gap is critical for developing models that accurately reflect human pathophysiology and for identifying mechanistically relevant treatment targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific plasma/serum proteomic alterations identified in human CHR individuals will be recapitulated in the plasma and brain tissue of subchronic PCP-treated mice exhibiting NOR deficits, and that pharmacological rescue of the NOR deficit (e.g., with rapastinel or NU-1223) will normalize these biomarker levels.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a targeted meta-analysis of pre-2025 human studies to define a consensus panel of candidate protein biomarkers (e.g., inflammatory markers, BDNF, complement factors) altered in early psychosis. Subchronic PCP-treated C57BL/6 mice will be assessed for NOR deficits. Plasma and prefrontal cortex samples from PCP-treated, vehicle-treated, and drug-rescued (rapastinel, NU-1223) mice will be analyzed using multiplex immunoassay (Luminex) for the candidate proteins. Statistical integration will use Partial Least Squares-Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) to identify biomarker patterns that distinguish groups and correlate with NOR performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, biomarker-based validation of the PCP-NOR model's relevance to human early psychosis biology. It will establish a framework for using human-derived molecular signatures to assess and refine rodent models of schizophrenia. Furthermore, it will generate novel data on the molecular mechanisms underlying the rescue effects of promising glutamatergic and serotonergic agents.
proposals.novelty: While biomarker discovery and rodent modeling proceed in parallel, this proposal directly integrates pre-2026 human proteomic findings into the validation of a specific cognitive model, moving beyond behavioral correlation to a molecular level of cross-species construct validation. The use of rescue paradigms with mechanistically distinct compounds (rapastinel/NU-1223) to probe biomarker state-dependency is also novel.

proposals.title: A Convergent Triad Pipeline for Identifying Novel Pro-Cognitive Agents in Schizophrenia: High-Throughput NOR Screening with Orthogonal Synaptic and Complex Behavioral Validation
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia are a primary determinant of functional disability and remain largely untreated by current antipsychotics, including clozapine. While simplified behavioral assays like the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) test in phencyclidine (PCP)-treated mice are valuable for high-throughput screening, they risk identifying compounds that improve performance through non-specific mechanisms, limiting translational success. There is a critical need for a more rigorous, multi-tiered screening strategy that couples initial behavioral hits with direct mechanistic and complex behavioral validation.
proposals.hypothesis: A screening pipeline that integrates a primary high-throughput NOR assay with parallel medium-throughput assessment of synaptic plasticity markers in neuronal cultures, followed by validation in a high-complexity spatial or associative memory task, will identify novel compounds with robust, mechanistically grounded pro-cognitive efficacy superior to those identified by NOR alone.
proposals.method: The pipeline has three convergent tiers. First, a miniaturized, automated NOR assay using the subchronic PCP mouse model will rapidly screen compound libraries. All primary hits will immediately enter a parallel in vitro tier using primary cortical or hippocampal neuron cultures; synaptic effects will be quantified via high-content imaging of PSD-95 puncta density and dendritic spine morphology. Finally, compounds showing convergent NOR rescue and synaptic enhancement will be tested in the low-throughput, high-complexity Morris water maze within the same PCP model, assessing spatial learning and memory.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, multi-modal pipeline for the discovery of pro-cognitive pharmacotherapies, directly addressing a major unmet need in schizophrenia. It will generate a shortlist of high-confidence candidate compounds with documented efficacy across behavioral and cellular domains of cognition. The pipeline itself will serve as a new standard for preclinical cognitive enhancer development, increasing predictive validity for clinical translation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the mandatory, parallel convergence of the three tiers—behavioral, cellular, and complex behavioral—applied to all primary hits, rather than sequential filtering. This 'triad' approach ensures selected compounds have a direct synaptic mechanism and efficacy beyond a single, potentially confounded, behavioral readout, a strategy not systematically employed in pre-2026 schizophrenia drug discovery.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a High-Throughput, Automated Novel Object Recognition (NOR) Assay for Rapid Screening of Cognitive Enhancers in a Phencyclidine Mouse Model of Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia remain a major unmet therapeutic need. Current drug discovery relies on low-throughput behavioral tests like the NOR, which is labor-intensive and limits the pace of screening novel compounds, such as the 5-HT2cR-targeting agents and rapamycin-related modulators (e.g., rapastinel) of interest to our group. A scalable, automated NOR platform is urgently needed to efficiently evaluate large libraries of potential cognitive-enhancing compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a miniaturized, automated NOR paradigm, utilizing video tracking and machine learning for object interaction scoring, will maintain the validity and sensitivity of the traditional NOR test while enabling high-throughput screening of compound libraries for efficacy against phencyclidine-induced cognitive deficits.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-chamber apparatus for parallel testing of 12 mice simultaneously. Behavior will be recorded via overhead cameras and analyzed using EthoVision XT software for initial tracking. A convolutional neural network (CNN), specifically a ResNet-50 architecture pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on manually annotated mouse-object interaction frames, will be implemented to classify exploration behavior with high accuracy. The system will be validated against manual scoring and tested for its ability to detect the pro-cognitive effects of positive controls (e.g., rapastinel, NU-1223) in the subchronic phencyclidine mouse model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a validated, open-source pipeline for high-throughput cognitive screening, dramatically accelerating the discovery phase for novel therapeutics targeting cognitive dysfunction in schizophrenia. It will directly enable the rapid prioritization of lead compounds from our ongoing drug development efforts for in-depth behavioral and mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: While automated home-cage monitoring exists, the direct adaptation of the standardized NOR test—a cornerstone for assessing recognition memory in schizophrenia models—into a validated, miniaturized, high-throughput format integrated with deep learning-based analysis is a novel and transformative methodological advance.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Profiling of Cognitive Resilience to NMDAR Hypofunction from Adolescence to Adulthood: A Novel Object Recognition Challenge Paradigm in Mice
proposals.motivation: Cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, linked to NMDAR hypofunction, are core drivers of disability and remain poorly treated. While cross-sectional models of NMDAR antagonism (e.g., phencyclidine, MK-801) are standard, they fail to capture the dynamic, developmental nature of cognitive vulnerability and resilience observed clinically. A longitudinal model that tracks individual cognitive trajectories under repeated challenge is needed to understand how resilience emerges or deteriorates across key neurodevelopmental windows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual mice will exhibit distinct, stable longitudinal trajectories of cognitive performance in response to repeated NMDAR antagonist challenge, and that a subset will demonstrate acquired or stable resilience, the neurobiology of which can be distinguished from persistent vulnerability.
proposals.method: C57BL/6J mice (n=60) will undergo a longitudinal Novel Object Recognition (NOR) protocol from postnatal day (P) 35 (adolescence) through P120 (adulthood). At five key timepoints (P35, P50, P70, P90, P120), each subject will complete a baseline NOR test followed 48 hours later by an identical NOR test conducted 30 minutes after a subcutaneous challenge with a low, sub-psychotomimetic dose of MK-801 (0.1 mg/kg). This yields paired baseline and challenge discrimination indices (DI) per subject per timepoint. Trajectories will be classified via growth mixture modeling to identify resilient, vulnerable, and acquired-resilience subgroups. Post-mortem analyses in these phenotyped groups will focus on prefrontal and hippocampal markers implicated in NMDAR function and cognitive reserve (e.g., GluN1, PSD-95, parvalbumin interneuron integrity).
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel, dynamic preclinical model of cognitive resilience to NMDAR hypofunction, moving beyond static deficit models. It will provide the first longitudinal map of individual cognitive trajectories under pharmacological stress, identifying distinct resilience phenotypes. The neurobiological correlates of these phenotypes will offer new targets for pro-cognitive interventions aimed at enhancing resilience mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the repeated, within-subject NMDAR challenge across development, which dynamically maps resilience as a process rather than a static trait. This approach directly tests the ontogeny and stability of cognitive sparing mechanisms, a question inaccessible to standard cross-sectional designs.

ideas.title: A Multi-Hit, Systems-Level Model for Characterizing Progressive Cognitive Impairment
ideas.description: This study establishes a more translationally robust model of cognitive impairment by combining chronic, low-dose anticholinergic challenge with a secondary metabolic or neuroinflammatory insult to mimic multi-factorial disease etiology. The model's progression and system-wide pathophysiology are characterized longitudinally using a battery of behavioral tasks spanning multiple cognitive domains and correlated with in-vivo neurophysiological and neuroinflammatory biomarkers. This integrated approach aims to identify convergent mechanisms of dysfunction beyond the cholinergic system and test remediation strategies with broader mechanistic targets, thereby increasing predictive validity for complex human disorders.

ideas.title: Circuit-Specific Predictive Coding Remediation: A Translational Framework from NMDAR Dysfunction to Cognitive Treatment in Schizophrenia
ideas.description: This research program directly translates the NMDAR hypofunction model of schizophrenia into viable cognitive treatments by moving from a broad pharmacological analogy to a precise, circuit-based computational framework. It posits that NMDAR dysfunction manifests as quantifiable distortions in predictive coding within specific fronto-temporal and thalamocortical microcircuits, which can be modeled and measured. The core approach is to design adaptive cognitive tasks that explicitly target and recalibrate these distorted predictive algorithms, using real-time neurofeedback and EEG/MRS biomarkers to verify NMDAR-relevant synaptic engagement. This creates a closed-loop system where cognitive interventions are iteratively refined based on direct neural readouts of circuit-level target engagement, bridging the gap between molecular hypothesis and cognitive remediation.

ideas.title: From Pathogen Model to Host-Directed Therapy: Adapting a Virology Strategy to NMDAR Dysfunction
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that the NMDAR antagonist model directly translates to viable cognitive treatments by drawing an analogy from virology. In antiviral research, directly targeting a rapidly mutating pathogen often fails, leading to the solution of host-directed therapy—targeting the stable host machinery the pathogen needs. Analogously, directly pharmacologically correcting NMDAR hypofunction has proven difficult. The adapted research idea is to shift focus from the 'pathogen' (the dysfunctional receptor) to the 'host' (the broader cognitive system). The solution is to identify and therapeutically strengthen stable, alternative neural circuits or cognitive algorithms that are not dependent on optimal NMDAR function but can compensate for the noise it introduces, using mechanistically guided cognitive training or non-invasive brain stimulation. This host-directed cognitive therapy approach aims for more robust and generalizable cognitive improvements in schizophrenia.

ideas.title: Longitudinal tracking of cognitive resilience to NMDAR antagonism across the lifespan
ideas.description: This study implements a longitudinal Novel Object Recognition (NOR) protocol from adolescence through adulthood in rodents, but with the critical addition of periodic, controlled challenges with NMDAR antagonists (e.g., MK-801). By repeatedly testing cognitive performance both at baseline and under pharmacological challenge in the same animals, the design dynamically maps individual trajectories of vulnerability and resilience. This allows for the identification of subjects that consistently resist cognitive deficits (stable resilient), those that develop resistance over time (acquired resilient), and those that become vulnerable only at specific developmental stages. The longitudinal challenge paradigm thus uncovers the ontogeny and stability of cognitive sparing mechanisms, providing a powerful model to probe the neurobiological correlates of resilience as it unfolds or deteriorates across the lifespan.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates proteomic/metabolomic biomarker signatures from human clinical high-risk and first-episode psychosis studies to specifically validate rodent models of neurodevelopmental or inflammatory insults. The core innovation is to test whether these rodent models not only show cognitive deficits in tasks like NOR, but also sequentially reproduce the human biomarker trajectory from the upstream insult (e.g., inflammation) through to the downstream glutamatergic/GABAergic dysfunction. This creates a causal, stage-specific validation pipeline, allowing the identification of which early-life insults in rodents best mirror the human pathophysiological cascade, thereby bridging biomarker discovery with mechanistic neurodevelopmental etiology.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Theory to Characterize and Remediate Scopolamine-Induced Cognitive Network Fragility
ideas.description: This idea applies the framework of ecological resilience theory to the problem of scopolamine-induced NOR deficits. In ecology, stressed ecosystems are analyzed for tipping points and loss of functional redundancy, with solutions aimed at restoring keystone components and network connectivity to rebuild systemic resilience. Adapted here, the research would characterize the cognitive impairment by defining the precise pharmacological 'tipping point' for network collapse and assessing the redundancy of memory encoding pathways. The core remediation hypothesis shifts from simple receptor antagonism reversal to testing whether enhancing overall neural network resilience—through means like environmental enrichment, physical exercise, or adjunctive neuromodulators (e.g., BDNF-promoting agents)—can not only reverse the deficit but also make the cognitive network more robust to subsequent cholinergic challenge.

ideas.title: Precision stratification of cognitive-psychosis causal pathways to optimize functional recovery
ideas.description: This research program first uses intensive longitudinal monitoring (e.g., digital phenotyping, cognitive sampling, EMA) in early psychosis to model individual-level causal relationships between cognitive performance and psychotic symptoms over time. Participants are then stratified into subgroups based on whether their data suggests a cognition-leading, psychosis-leading, or tightly coupled pathway. In a subsequent experimental trial, these subgroups are randomized to receive a matched intervention (e.g., cognitive remediation for cognition-leading, antipsychotic optimization for psychosis-leading, combined for coupled), testing the hypothesis that functional outcomes are superior when the treatment target aligns with the individual's primary causal pathway.

ideas.title: Adaptive GABAergic Circuit Tuning via Allosteric Modulators
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from wireless communication networks, where adaptive algorithms dynamically tune signal transmission to correct for interference and optimize network performance, this research idea adapts that solution to correct GABAergic dysfunction. The analogous problem of non-specific signal adjustment is solved via targeted, feedback-dependent modulation. The adapted idea is to develop and test novel activity-dependent allosteric modulators of GABA-A receptors. These compounds would not have a blanket effect but would selectively modulate GABAergic transmission based on the real-time activity levels of the specific neural circuits dysregulated in NMDAR antagonist rodent models, thereby providing a precise, circuit-specific pharmacological strategy to remediate NOR deficits by retuning the excitation-inhibition balance.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Circuit Breaker' Approach from Electrical Engineering to Map NOR Deficits in Schizophrenia Models
ideas.description: This project adapts a fault-isolation strategy from electrical engineering to systematically investigate NOR deficits in schizophrenia-relevant transgenic mice. The analogous solution from circuit design involves mapping a system and using 'circuit breakers' to pinpoint failures. Adapted here, we will first define the canonical neural circuit modules for NOR. Then, within established transgenic mouse models (e.g., with mutations in DISC1, NRG1, or 22q11.2 orthologs), we will employ cell-type-specific, reversible interventions (chemogenetic silencing/activation) to disrupt or rescue activity in specific nodes (e.g., perirhinal cortex, ventral hippocampus, medial prefrontal cortex). This approach will test which genetic lesion requires disruption of which specific circuit component to manifest the behavioral NOR impairment, thereby dissecting the causal pathway from schizophrenia-risk gene to circuit dysfunction to cognitive symptom.

ideas.title: Define the necessary and sufficient causal network for ketamine's rapid antidepressant action
ideas.description: This project aims to map the causal network of molecular, cellular, and circuit-level events required for ketamine's rapid antidepressant effects, moving beyond a single trigger. It employs closed-loop experimental systems in rodent models that combine targeted perturbations of multiple candidate pathways with simultaneous real-time monitoring of synaptic plasticity and network dynamics in mood-relevant circuits. The resulting high-dimensional data is analyzed using causal inference and machine learning to derive a predictive model of the minimal interacting components that produce the therapeutic outcome, thereby identifying robust control points for intervention.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Computational Framework Linking Ketamine's Molecular Actions to Episodic Memory Network Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational models of episodic memory (e.g., hippocampal-cortical pattern separation/completion) with a multi-level biological model of ketamine's action. Instead of analyzing NOR data with simple discrimination indices, the framework uses the computational memory model to generate quantitative predictions for behavioral performance (e.g., pattern separation fidelity, memory integration errors) based on simulated network states. These network states are directly parameterized by the downstream cellular and synaptic consequences (e.g., disinhibition, altered plasticity windows) of ketamine's primary molecular pharmacology. This allows for causal, mechanistic tracing of a drug's effect from molecule to specific cognitive computation, moving beyond descriptive correlations at any single level.

ideas.title: A Convergent Triad Pipeline: High-Throughput NOR Screening with Orthogonal Physiological and Complex Behavioral Validation
ideas.description: This approach first uses an automated, miniaturized NOR assay for rapid primary screening of compound libraries. Crucially, all hits from this screen are then evaluated in a parallel, medium-throughput in vitro assay quantifying direct effects on synaptic markers or plasticity in relevant neuronal cultures. Finally, the most promising compounds from this convergent filter undergo validation in a low-throughput, high-complexity behavioral paradigm (e.g., Morris water maze or contextual fear conditioning) within an established disease model. This tiered system ensures selected candidates have robust, mechanistically supported pro-cognitive effects beyond a single, simplified behavioral readout.

ideas.title: Causal pathway validation of rodent NOR using conserved dysregulated networks from human CHR states
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea moves beyond simple biomarker correlation to establish causal, conserved pathway validity for the NOR model. It integrates multi-omics data from human Clinical High Risk studies to identify dysregulated molecular networks and then tests their necessity and sufficiency for NOR performance in rodents via precise genetic and pharmacological manipulations. The approach validates the model not by matching static biomarkers, but by demonstrating that perturbation of evolutionarily conserved pathways, identified in human early illness, produces predictable and reversible deficits in the rodent cognitive assay. This creates a robust, mechanistically grounded link between the model system and the human condition it aims to represent.

ideas.title: Causal interrogation of a memory circuit linking in vivo behavior to ex vivo synaptic necessity
ideas.description: This study causally tests whether synaptic plasticity in a defined circuit is necessary for Novel Object Recognition (NOR) memory. Following NOR training, the specific pathway (e.g., perirhinal-hippocampal) is acutely and reversibly inhibited in vivo using optogenetics during the memory consolidation window. Memory performance is assessed 24 hours later. Immediately after behavioral testing, brain slices are prepared to measure synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD) ex vivo in the same manipulated pathway from these animals. This design directly links the necessity of the circuit for the behavior to the state of its synaptic plasticity, moving beyond correlation to establish a mechanistic bridge.

ideas.title: Targeting Cognitive Infrastructure to Improve Functional Outcomes in Psychosis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cognitive impairment is merely secondary to psychosis by drawing an analogy from computer systems engineering. In that field, system-wide failures are often addressed not by solely targeting the most visible error signal but by profiling and remediating underlying resource bottlenecks (like memory or bandwidth), which improves overall performance even if primary errors persist. Adapted to psychiatry, this suggests a research program where cognitive impairment is treated as the fundamental 'bottleneck' limiting functional recovery. Clinical trials would prioritize direct cognitive remediation or pro-cognitive agents as the primary intervention, hypothesizing that enhancing cognitive 'throughput' will lead to superior and more independent gains in real-world functioning than treatments targeting psychotic symptoms alone, thereby testing the causal primacy of cognitive deficits.

ideas.title: Tracing Cognitive Deficits to Upstream 'Circuit Breaker' Failures Using Systems Ecology Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cognitive deficits are primarily a result of neurotransmitter dysfunction. By drawing an analogy from systems ecology, where species collapse is traced to upstream 'regime shifts,' we propose to investigate cognitive deficits as the downstream consequence of upstream neurodevelopmental or inflammatory 'regime shifts' in the brain's network. The adapted solution involves using computational systems modeling to map the brain's connectome and signaling pathways, aiming to identify critical tipping points (e.g., specific inflammatory cascades or aberrant pruning events) that precede and cause the glutamatergic and GABAergic deficits. This shifts the research focus from treating the neurotransmitter imbalance as the primary pathology to preventing or intervening at the earlier, systemic regime shift.

ideas.title: A Composite Cognitive Battery Using NOR as a Core Module, Modeled on Ecological Health Indices
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from environmental science, where composite indices (like the Ocean Health Index) integrate diverse, standardized metrics to assess overall ecosystem health. The analogous problem of needing a holistic assessment from multiple data streams is solved by creating modular, weighted indices. Adapted to cognitive neuroscience, the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) test becomes one core module within a broader, standardized cognitive battery. This battery would include other brief, computerized tests targeting domains impaired in schizophrenia (e.g., working memory with N-back, attention with CPT, executive function with task-switching). The data from all modules are integrated algorithmically to generate both a domain-specific profile and a composite 'Cognitive Integrity Index,' offering a more nuanced, efficient, and comprehensive tool for tracking cognitive dysfunction and treatment response in schizophrenia, mirroring how ecologists track ecosystem recovery.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Systems Biology Framework for Ketamine's Mechanism of Action
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ketamine's actions are best understood at a single biological level by drawing an analogy from climate science. Climate scientists solved the analogous problem of understanding a complex, emergent phenomenon (climate change) by developing Earth System Models that integrate processes across all relevant scales and subsystems. The adapted research idea is to develop a computational 'Ketamine System Model' (KSM). This model would formally integrate dynamic data from molecular, cellular, and network levels into a single framework to simulate interventions and identify the critical cross-level interactions responsible for ketamine's rapid antidepressant and dissociative effects, moving beyond isolated level-specific explanations.

ideas.title: Circuit-phase-specific silencing of projection-defined neuronal ensembles in NOR
ideas.description: This approach uses Cre- and Flp-dependent viral vectors to target designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) to neurons defined by both their genetic identity and their long-range axonal projections. By administering the inert ligand at precise timepoints (e.g., pre-sample, post-sample delay, or during test), the system can reversibly silence specific microcircuits during distinct cognitive phases of the Novel Object Recognition task. This enables a high-resolution functional dissection of which information streams are necessary for novelty detection versus familiarity recall, moving beyond regional necessity to define circuit-level algorithmic necessity.

ideas.title: High-throughput neurophenotypic screening: Integrating real-time circuit readouts into automated NOR for mechanism-based drug discovery
ideas.description: This research direction develops an integrated system where rodents perform the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) task in a fully automated, high-throughput arena while undergoing simultaneous, minimally invasive neural recording (e.g., via chronically implanted micro-EEG arrays or miniaturized wide-field fluorescence microscopes). Each tested compound from a large library yields a paired dataset: standard behavioral readout (discrimination index) and a rich, time-locked neural dynamic profile (e.g., prefrontal-hippocampal coherence, novelty-evoked gamma power). Machine learning pipelines then analyze these fused datasets to cluster compounds by their 'neurophenotypic' impact, directly linking cognitive enhancement to the correction of specific circuit dysfunctions, thereby accelerating the transition from phenotypic hit to mechanistically understood candidate.

ideas.title: A Multi-Process Computational Architecture to Decompose and Localize Cognitive Deficits in Novel Object Recognition
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a process-level computational model of the NOR task that decomposes performance into interacting sub-systems for perception, exploration, and episodic memory. The model is fit to high-resolution behavioral data (e.g., from video tracking and touch sensors) to estimate individual subject parameters for each cognitive process. This allows quantitative differentiation between, for example, a recognition deficit caused by poor perceptual encoding versus true episodic memory failure, providing a more precise and mechanistically informative biomarker for neurological disease models than a simple discrimination index.

ideas.title: Live-Cell Photaffinity Chemoproteomics to Map Ketamine's Functional Off-Target Engagements in Neurons
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a photoreactive, clickable ketamine analog to capture the drug's protein interactions within the native environment of living, functioning neurons. By performing photo-crosslinking on intact cells, it preserves interactions that depend on physiological membrane potentials, ion concentrations, and specific protein conformations lost in lysate-based assays. Subsequent chemoproteomic analysis of captured targets identifies off-binding sites relevant to ketamine's acute pharmacological and side-effect profiles. This yields a functionally relevant map of ketamine's molecular engagements that directly informs its complex neurobiological effects.

ideas.title: Adapt Circuit-Breaker Logic from Electrical Grids for Pharmacogenetic Silencing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'circuit-breaker' approach from electrical grid engineering to neuroscience. In power grids, targeted, reversible breakers are used to isolate components and map their role in network stability. Adapted here, we propose using cell-type-specific pharmacogenetic tools (e.g., DREADDs) to reversibly silence individual brain regions implicated in the Novel Object Recognition (NOR) memory circuit. By applying these 'silencing breakers' in a sequential, temporally precise manner while monitoring neural activity and behavior, we can map the necessity of each node and, crucially, observe the real-time cascade of functional disconnection and potential compensatory rerouting within the broader memory network, providing a dynamic map of circuit necessity and resilience.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Touchscreen-Based Cognitive Trajectory Mapping in Rodents
ideas.description: This project integrates automated touchscreen cognitive testing with a longitudinal design to map the developmental trajectory of complex declarative memory in rodents from adolescence through old age. By repeatedly testing the same animals on a battery of touchscreen tasks—such as paired-associate learning, location-object association, and pattern separation—the study creates a continuous, high-dimensional dataset of cognitive function for each subject. This approach allows for the identification of sensitive periods for specific cognitive domains and the precise detection of when and how different memory systems begin to diverge from a healthy trajectory. The resulting longitudinal cognitive profiles provide a powerful, automated framework for testing interventions aimed at preventing or delaying domain-specific cognitive decline.

ideas.title: Enhancing NOR via Signal-to-Noise Optimization in Neural Circuits
ideas.description: This idea applies the signal-to-noise optimization principle from telecommunications to the neuroscience of declarative memory. The analogous problem of enhancing a signal in a noisy channel is solved by filtering noise and using matched detectors. Adapted here, the research would investigate whether boosting Novel Object Recognition (NOR) performance in healthy rodents has an upper limit defined not by the maximum strength of a memory trace, but by the optimal fidelity of its neural representation. This would be tested by combining cognitive enhancers with interventions designed to reduce neural noise or interference (e.g., via targeted inhibition of parvalbumin interneurons to sharpen cortical representations or using adrenergic agonists to enhance attention-based filtering) during memory encoding, thereby isolating and maximizing the 'signal' of the novel object.

ideas.title: Species-Centered, Multi-Modal Declarative Memory Assay for Rodents with Validated Touchscreen Response
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a rodent cognitive testing system that moves beyond a direct primate-to-rodent port. It first implements a suite of psychophysical validation tasks on the touchscreen to quantify and account for species-specific visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and motor patterns. The core declarative memory task then uses a multi-modal encoding phase, where the to-be-remembered sample is presented as a compound stimulus combining a visual touchscreen element with a congruent non-visual cue (e.g., a specific odor). The rodent subsequently makes its declarative choice via the touchscreen, ensuring the measured performance reflects integrated memory recall rather than a purely visual-motor association. This design rigorously controls for perceptual and motor confounds to provide a more robust and interpretable measure of complex declarative memory.

ideas.title: Longitudinal NOR with Adaptive Task Scaling via Psychometric Principles
ideas.description: This expanded idea addresses the core challenge of the original longitudinal NOR test—how to validly measure the same cognitive construct (recognition memory) across a lifespan where performance on a single, fixed task may hit ceiling effects in youth and floor effects in age. Drawing an analogy from developmental psychometrics, the solution is to adapt the method of 'vertical scaling' used in human testing. In the analogous field, tests for different age groups are linked via common items or statistical models (Item Response Theory) to place scores on a single, continuous ability scale. Adapted to the rodent context, this involves developing a bank of NOR object pairs with pre-calibrated discrimination difficulties (e.g., varying object similarity, exploration time, or retention interval). During longitudinal testing from adolescence to adulthood and senescence, the specific task parameters or object pairs are adaptively selected based on the animal's prior performance. The resulting data is then analyzed using psychometric models to estimate each animal's recognition memory 'ability' on a common scale at each time point. This allows for the true tracking of cognitive trajectory, separating practice effects from developmental or degenerative change, and providing a more sensitive and valid metric of lifespan cognitive decline than raw NOR discrimination indices from a static task.

ideas.title: Causal sequencing of insult-to-circuit pathology using longitudinal multi-modal phenotyping
ideas.description: This study will determine the causal and temporal hierarchy of events leading to cognitive deficits by modeling sequential insults in a controlled system. It moves beyond correlation by using precise developmental manipulations followed by inflammatory challenges, with continuous monitoring of neural circuit activity and immune markers. The core innovation is the integration of temporal phenotyping to distinguish whether neurotransmitter dysfunction is a direct consequence of an upstream insult, a parallel pathology, or a necessary intermediary for final cognitive impairment. This approach will generate a mechanistic map, rather than a list of associations, defining the primary pathological sequence.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for Circuit Mapping in Rodent NOR
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of tracking circuit dynamics during the NOR task in rodent models. In seismology, the solution involves using waves from distributed earthquakes recorded by sensor networks to computationally reconstruct 3D images of subsurface structures. Adapted to neuroscience, this involves using tools like optogenetics to induce controlled, focal neural 'perturbations' (analogous to micro-earthquakes) within specific circuits of a behaving rodent during the NOR task. The propagated activity is recorded via a high-density EEG array or functional imaging, and computational tomography algorithms are then applied to this data. This allows for the reconstruction of dynamic, task-dependent functional connectivity maps, revealing how information flows and circuits reconfigure during the encoding and retrieval phases of object recognition memory.
